**New Zealand** ([Māori](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language%20%22M%C4%81ori%20language%22): _[Aotearoa](http://fakehost/wiki/Aotearoa%20%22Aotearoa%22)_ [\[aɔˈtɛaɾɔa\]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori")) is a [sovereign](http://fakehost/wiki/Sovereign_state%20%22Sovereign%20state%22) [island country](http://fakehost/wiki/Island_country%20%22Island%20country%22) in the southwestern [Pacific Ocean](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Ocean%20%22Pacific%20Ocean%22). The country geographically comprises two main landmasses—the [North Island](http://fakehost/wiki/North_Island%20%22North%20Island%22) (_Te Ika-a-Māui_), and the [South Island](http://fakehost/wiki/South_Island%20%22South%20Island%22) (_Te Waipounamu_)—and around 600 [smaller islands](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_islands_of_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20islands%20of%20New%20Zealand%22). It has a total land area of 268,000 square kilometres (103,500 sq mi). New Zealand is situated some 2,000 kilometres (1,200 mi) east of [Australia](http://fakehost/wiki/Australia%20%22Australia%22) across the [Tasman Sea](http://fakehost/wiki/Tasman_Sea%20%22Tasman%20Sea%22) and roughly 1,000 kilometres (600 mi) south of the [Pacific island areas](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_islands_in_the_Pacific_Ocean%20%22List%20of%20islands%20in%20the%20Pacific%20Ocean%22) of [New Caledonia](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Caledonia%20%22New%20Caledonia%22), [Fiji](http://fakehost/wiki/Fiji%20%22Fiji%22), and [Tonga](http://fakehost/wiki/Tonga%20%22Tonga%22). Because of its remoteness, it was one of the last lands to be settled by humans. During its long period of isolation, New Zealand developed a distinct [biodiversity](http://fakehost/wiki/Biodiversity_of_New_Zealand%20%22Biodiversity%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) of animal, fungal, and plant life. The country's varied topography and its sharp mountain peaks, such as the [Southern Alps](http://fakehost/wiki/Southern_Alps%20%22Southern%20Alps%22), owe much to the [tectonic uplift](http://fakehost/wiki/Tectonic_uplift%20%22Tectonic%20uplift%22) of land and volcanic eruptions. New Zealand's [capital city](http://fakehost/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand%20%22Capital%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) is [Wellington](http://fakehost/wiki/Wellington%20%22Wellington%22), while its most populous city is [Auckland](http://fakehost/wiki/Auckland%20%22Auckland%22).

Sometime between 1250 and 1300, [Polynesians](http://fakehost/wiki/Polynesians%20%22Polynesians%22) settled in the islands that later were named New Zealand and developed a distinctive [Māori culture](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture%20%22M%C4%81ori%20culture%22). In 1642, Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](http://fakehost/wiki/Abel_Tasman%20%22Abel%20Tasman%22) became the first European to sight New Zealand. In 1840, representatives of the United Kingdom and [Māori](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people%20%22M%C4%81ori%20people%22) chiefs signed the [Treaty of Waitangi](http://fakehost/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi%20%22Treaty%20of%20Waitangi%22), which declared British sovereignty over the islands. In 1841, New Zealand [became a colony](http://fakehost/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand%20%22Colony%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) within the [British Empire](http://fakehost/wiki/British_Empire%20%22British%20Empire%22) and in 1907 it [became a dominion](http://fakehost/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand%20%22Dominion%20of%20New%20Zealand%22); it gained [full statutory independence](http://fakehost/wiki/Independence_of_New_Zealand%20%22Independence%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) in 1947 and the British monarch remained the [head of state](http://fakehost/wiki/Head_of_state%20%22Head%20of%20state%22). Today, the majority of [New Zealand's population](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand%27s_population%20%22New%20Zealand's%20population%22) of 4.9 million is of [European descent](http://fakehost/wiki/European_New_Zealanders%20%22European%20New%20Zealanders%22); the indigenous Māori are the largest minority, followed by [Asians](http://fakehost/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders%20%22Asian%20New%20Zealanders%22) and [Pacific Islanders](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Islander%20%22Pacific%20Islander%22). Reflecting this, [New Zealand's culture](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand%27s_culture%20%22New%20Zealand's%20culture%22) is mainly derived from Māori and early British settlers, with recent broadening arising from increased [immigration](http://fakehost/wiki/Immigration_to_New_Zealand%20%22Immigration%20to%20New%20Zealand%22). The [official languages](http://fakehost/wiki/Languages_of_New_Zealand%20%22Languages%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) are [English](http://fakehost/wiki/English_language%20%22English%20language%22), [Māori](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language%20%22M%C4%81ori%20language%22), and [New Zealand Sign Language](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language%20%22New%20Zealand%20Sign%20Language%22), with English being very dominant.

A [developed country](http://fakehost/wiki/Developed_country%20%22Developed%20country%22), New Zealand [ranks highly](http://fakehost/wiki/International_rankings_of_New_Zealand%20%22International%20rankings%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) in international comparisons of national performance, such as quality of life, health, education, protection of [civil liberties](http://fakehost/wiki/Civil_liberties%20%22Civil%20liberties%22), and [economic freedom](http://fakehost/wiki/Economic_freedom%20%22Economic%20freedom%22). New Zealand underwent [major economic changes](http://fakehost/wiki/Rogernomics%20%22Rogernomics%22) during the 1980s, which transformed it from a [protectionist](http://fakehost/wiki/Protectionist%20%22Protectionist%22) to a [liberalised](http://fakehost/wiki/Economic_liberalization%20%22Economic%20liberalization%22) [free-trade](http://fakehost/wiki/Free-trade%20%22Free-trade%22) economy. The service sector dominates the [national economy](http://fakehost/wiki/Economy_of_New_Zealand%20%22Economy%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), followed by the industrial sector, and [agriculture](http://fakehost/wiki/Agriculture_in_New_Zealand%20%22Agriculture%20in%20New%20Zealand%22); international [tourism](http://fakehost/wiki/Tourism_in_New_Zealand%20%22Tourism%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) is a significant source of revenue. Nationally, legislative authority is vested in an elected, [unicameral](http://fakehost/wiki/Unicameral%20%22Unicameral%22) [Parliament](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament%20%22New%20Zealand%20Parliament%22), while executive political power is exercised by the [Cabinet](http://fakehost/wiki/Cabinet_of_New_Zealand%20%22Cabinet%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), led by the [prime minister](http://fakehost/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand%20%22Prime%20Minister%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), currently [Jacinda Ardern](http://fakehost/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern%20%22Jacinda%20Ardern%22). [Queen Elizabeth II](http://fakehost/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II%20%22Queen%20Elizabeth%20II%22) is the [country's monarch](http://fakehost/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand%20%22Monarchy%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) and is represented by a [governor-general](http://fakehost/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand%20%22Governor-General%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), currently [Dame Patsy Reddy](http://fakehost/wiki/Dame_Patsy_Reddy%20%22Dame%20Patsy%20Reddy%22). In addition, New Zealand is organised into 11 [regional councils](http://fakehost/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand%20%22Regions%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) and 67 [territorial authorities](http://fakehost/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand%20%22Territorial%20authorities%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) for local government purposes. The [Realm of New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/Realm_of_New_Zealand%20%22Realm%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) also includes [Tokelau](http://fakehost/wiki/Tokelau%20%22Tokelau%22) (a [dependent territory](http://fakehost/wiki/Dependent_territory%20%22Dependent%20territory%22)); the [Cook Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Cook_Islands%20%22Cook%20Islands%22) and [Niue](http://fakehost/wiki/Niue%20%22Niue%22) (self-governing states in [free association](http://fakehost/wiki/Associated_state%20%22Associated%20state%22) with New Zealand); and the [Ross Dependency](http://fakehost/wiki/Ross_Dependency%20%22Ross%20Dependency%22), which is New Zealand's [territorial claim in Antarctica](http://fakehost/wiki/Territorial_claim_in_Antarctica%20%22Territorial%20claim%20in%20Antarctica%22). New Zealand is a member of the [United Nations](http://fakehost/wiki/United_Nations%20%22United%20Nations%22), [Commonwealth of Nations](http://fakehost/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations%20%22Commonwealth%20of%20Nations%22), [ANZUS](http://fakehost/wiki/ANZUS%20%22ANZUS%22), [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](http://fakehost/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development%20%22Organisation%20for%20Economic%20Co-operation%20and%20Development%22), [ASEAN Plus Six](http://fakehost/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations#ASEAN_Plus_Three_and_Six%20%22Association%20of%20Southeast%20Asian%20Nations%22), [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](http://fakehost/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation%20%22Asia-Pacific%20Economic%20Cooperation%22), the [Pacific Community](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Community%20%22Pacific%20Community%22) and the [Pacific Islands Forum](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Forum%20%22Pacific%20Islands%20Forum%22).

Etymology
---------

[![Brown square paper with Dutch writing and a thick red, curved line](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Detail_of_1657_map_Polus_Antarcticus_by_Jan_Janssonius%2C_showing_Nova_Zeelandia.png/220px-Detail_of_1657_map_Polus_Antarcticus_by_Jan_Janssonius%2C_showing_Nova_Zeelandia.png)](/wiki/File:Detail_of_1657_map_Polus_Antarcticus_by_Jan_Janssonius,_showing_Nova_Zeelandia.png)

Detail from a 1657 map showing the western coastline of "Nova Zeelandia". (In this map, north is at the bottom.)

[Dutch](http://fakehost/wiki/Netherlands%20%22Netherlands%22) explorer Abel Tasman sighted New Zealand in 1642 and named it _Staten Land_ "in honour of the [States General](http://fakehost/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands%20%22States%20General%20of%20the%20Netherlands%22)" (Dutch parliament). He wrote, "it is possible that this land joins to the Staten Land but it is uncertain",[\[11\]](#cite_note-17) referring to [a landmass of the same name](http://fakehost/wiki/Isla_de_los_Estados%20%22Isla%20de%20los%20Estados%22) at the southern tip of South America, discovered by [Jacob Le Maire](http://fakehost/wiki/Jacob_Le_Maire%20%22Jacob%20Le%20Maire%22) in 1616.[\[12\]](#cite_note-18)
[\[13\]](#cite_note-19) In 1645, Dutch [cartographers](http://fakehost/wiki/Cartographers%20%22Cartographers%22) renamed the land _Nova Zeelandia_ after the [Dutch province](http://fakehost/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces%20%22Seventeen%20Provinces%22) of [Zeeland](http://fakehost/wiki/Zeeland%20%22Zeeland%22).[\[14\]](#cite_note-20)
[\[15\]](#cite_note-21) British explorer [James Cook](http://fakehost/wiki/James_Cook%20%22James%20Cook%22) subsequently [anglicised](http://fakehost/wiki/Anglicisation%20%22Anglicisation%22) the name to New Zealand.[\[16\]](#cite_note-NZ_name-22)

_[Aotearoa](http://fakehost/wiki/Aotearoa%20%22Aotearoa%22)_ (pronounced ; often translated as "land of the long white cloud")
[\[17\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing200341-23) is the current Māori name for New Zealand. It is unknown whether Māori had a name for the whole country before the arrival of Europeans, with _Aotearoa_ originally referring to just the [North Island](http://fakehost/wiki/North_Island%20%22North%20Island%22).[\[18\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayMaclaganGordon200872-24) Māori had several traditional names for the two main islands, including _Te Ika-a-Māui_ (the fish of [Māui](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ui_/(M%C4%81ori_mythology/) "Māui (Māori mythology)")) for the North Island and _Te Waipounamu_ (the waters of [greenstone](http://fakehost/wiki/Pounamu%20%22Pounamu%22)) or _Te Waka o Aoraki_ (the canoe of [Aoraki](http://fakehost/wiki/Aoraki%20%22Aoraki%22)) for the [South Island](http://fakehost/wiki/South_Island%20%22South%20Island%22).[\[19\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith20056-25) Early European maps labelled the islands North (North Island), Middle (South Island) and South ([Stewart Island / Rakiura](http://fakehost/wiki/Stewart_Island_/_Rakiura%20%22Stewart%20Island%20/%20Rakiura%22)).[\[20\]](#cite_note-26) In 1830, mapmakers began to use "North" and "South" on their maps to distinguish the two largest islands and by 1907 this was the accepted norm.[\[16\]](#cite_note-NZ_name-22) The [New Zealand Geographic Board](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Geographic_Board%20%22New%20Zealand%20Geographic%20Board%22) discovered in 2009 that the names of the North Island and South Island had never been formalised, and names and alternative names were formalised in 2013. This set the names as North Island or Te Ika-a-Māui, and South Island or Te Waipounamu.[\[21\]](#cite_note-Williamson2013-27) For each island, either its English or Māori name can be used, or both can be used together.[\[21\]](#cite_note-Williamson2013-27)

History
-------

[![One set of arrows point from Taiwan to Melanesia to Fiji/Samoa and then to the Marquesas Islands. The population then spread, some going south to New Zealand and others going north to Hawai'i. A second set start in southern Asia and end in Melanesia.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Polynesian_Migration.svg/290px-Polynesian_Migration.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Polynesian_Migration.svg)

The [Māori people](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people%20%22M%C4%81ori%20people%22) are most likely descended from people who emigrated from [Taiwan](http://fakehost/wiki/Taiwan%20%22Taiwan%22) to [Melanesia](http://fakehost/wiki/Melanesia%20%22Melanesia%22) and then travelled east through to the [Society Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Society_Islands%20%22Society%20Islands%22). After a pause of 70 to 265 years, a new wave of exploration led to the discovery and settlement of New Zealand.[\[22\]](#cite_note-28)

New Zealand was one of the last major landmasses settled by humans. [Radiocarbon dating](http://fakehost/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating%20%22Radiocarbon%20dating%22), evidence of [deforestation](http://fakehost/wiki/Deforestation%20%22Deforestation%22)
[\[23\]](#cite_note-29) and [mitochondrial DNA](http://fakehost/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA%20%22Mitochondrial%20DNA%22) variability within [Māori](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people%20%22M%C4%81ori%20people%22) populations[\[24\]](#cite_note-30) suggest New Zealand was first settled by Eastern [Polynesians](http://fakehost/wiki/Polynesians%20%22Polynesians%22) between 1250 and 1300,[\[19\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith20056-25)
[\[25\]](#cite_note-31) concluding a long series of voyages through the southern Pacific islands.[\[26\]](#cite_note-32) Over the centuries that followed, these settlers developed a distinct culture now known as Māori. The population was divided into _[iwi](http://fakehost/wiki/Iwi%20%22Iwi%22)_ (tribes) and _[hapū](http://fakehost/wiki/Hap%C5%AB%20%22Hap%C5%AB%22)_ (subtribes) who would sometimes cooperate, sometimes compete and sometimes fight against each other.[\[27\]](#cite_note-33) At some point a group of Māori migrated to _Rēkohu_, now known as the [Chatham Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Chatham_Islands%20%22Chatham%20Islands%22), where they developed their distinct [Moriori](http://fakehost/wiki/Moriori%20%22Moriori%22) culture.[\[28\]](#cite_note-34)
[\[29\]](#cite_note-35) The Moriori population was all but wiped out between 1835 and 1862, largely because of [Taranaki](http://fakehost/wiki/Taranaki_/(iwi/) "Taranaki (iwi)") Māori invasion and enslavement in the 1830s, although European diseases also contributed. In 1862 only 101 survived, and the last known full-blooded Moriori died in 1933.[\[30\]](#cite_note-36)

[![An engraving of a sketched coastline on white background](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Cook_chart_of_New_Zealand.jpg/170px-Cook_chart_of_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cook_chart_of_New_Zealand.jpg)

Map of the New Zealand coastline as Cook charted it on his [first visit](http://fakehost/wiki/First_voyage_of_James_Cook%20%22First%20voyage%20of%20James%20Cook%22) in 1769–70. The track of the _[Endeavour](http://fakehost/wiki/HMS_Endeavour%20%22HMS%20Endeavour%22)_ is also shown.

The first [Europeans](http://fakehost/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe%20%22Ethnic%20groups%20in%20Europe%22) known to have reached New Zealand were Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](http://fakehost/wiki/Abel_Tasman%20%22Abel%20Tasman%22) and his crew in 1642.[\[31\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith200523-37) In a hostile encounter, four crew members were killed and at least one Māori was hit by [canister shot](http://fakehost/wiki/Canister_shot%20%22Canister%20shot%22).[\[32\]](#cite_note-38) Europeans did not revisit New Zealand until 1769 when British explorer [James Cook](http://fakehost/wiki/James_Cook%20%22James%20Cook%22) mapped almost the entire coastline.[\[31\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith200523-37) Following Cook, New Zealand was visited by numerous European and North American [whaling](http://fakehost/wiki/History_of_whaling%20%22History%20of%20whaling%22), [sealing](http://fakehost/wiki/Seal_hunting%20%22Seal%20hunting%22) and trading ships. They traded European food, metal tools, weapons and other goods for timber, Māori food, artefacts and water.[\[33\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing2003122-39) The introduction of the potato and the [musket](http://fakehost/wiki/Musket%20%22Musket%22) transformed Māori agriculture and warfare. Potatoes provided a reliable food surplus, which enabled longer and more sustained military campaigns.[\[34\]](#cite_note-40) The resulting intertribal [Musket Wars](http://fakehost/wiki/Musket_Wars%20%22Musket%20Wars%22) encompassed over 600 battles between 1801 and 1840, killing 30,000–40,000 Māori.[\[35\]](#cite_note-41) From the early 19th century, Christian [missionaries](http://fakehost/wiki/Missionary%20%22Missionary%22) began to settle New Zealand, eventually [converting](http://fakehost/wiki/Religious_conversion%20%22Religious%20conversion%22) most of the Māori population.[\[36\]](#cite_note-42) The Māori population declined to around 40% of its pre-contact level during the 19th century; introduced diseases were the major factor.[\[37\]](#cite_note-43)

[![A torn sheet of paper](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Treatyofwaitangi.jpg/170px-Treatyofwaitangi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treatyofwaitangi.jpg)

In 1788 Captain [Arthur Phillip](http://fakehost/wiki/Arthur_Phillip%20%22Arthur%20Phillip%22) assumed the position of [Governor](http://fakehost/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales%20%22Governor%20of%20New%20South%20Wales%22) of the new British colony of [New South Wales](http://fakehost/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales%20%22Colony%20of%20New%20South%20Wales%22) which according to his commission included New Zealand.[\[38\]](#cite_note-44) The British Government appointed [James Busby](http://fakehost/wiki/James_Busby%20%22James%20Busby%22) as British Resident to New Zealand in 1832 following a petition from northern Māori.[\[39\]](#cite_note-Busby-45) In 1835, following an announcement of impending French settlement by [Charles de Thierry](http://fakehost/wiki/Charles_de_Thierry%20%22Charles%20de%20Thierry%22), the nebulous [United Tribes of New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/United_Tribes_of_New_Zealand%20%22United%20Tribes%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) sent a [Declaration of Independence](http://fakehost/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Independence_of_New_Zealand%20%22Declaration%20of%20the%20Independence%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) to King [William IV of the United Kingdom](http://fakehost/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom%20%22William%20IV%20of%20the%20United%20Kingdom%22) asking for protection.[\[39\]](#cite_note-Busby-45) Ongoing unrest, the proposed settlement of New Zealand by the [New Zealand Company](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Company%20%22New%20Zealand%20Company%22) (which had already sent its first ship of surveyors to buy land from Māori) and the dubious legal standing of the Declaration of Independence prompted the [Colonial Office](http://fakehost/wiki/Colonial_Office%20%22Colonial%20Office%22) to send Captain [William Hobson](http://fakehost/wiki/William_Hobson%20%22William%20Hobson%22) to claim sovereignty for the [United Kingdom](http://fakehost/wiki/United_Kingdom%20%22United%20Kingdom%22) and negotiate a treaty with the Māori.[\[40\]](#cite_note-46) The [Treaty of Waitangi](http://fakehost/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi%20%22Treaty%20of%20Waitangi%22) was first signed in the [Bay of Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Bay_of_Islands%20%22Bay%20of%20Islands%22) on 6 February 1840.[\[41\]](#cite_note-Wilson2009-47) In response to the New Zealand Company's attempts to establish an independent settlement in [Wellington](http://fakehost/wiki/Wellington%20%22Wellington%22)
[\[42\]](#cite_note-48) and French settlers purchasing land in [Akaroa](http://fakehost/wiki/Akaroa%20%22Akaroa%22),[\[43\]](#cite_note-49) Hobson declared British sovereignty over all of New Zealand on 21 May 1840, even though copies of the Treaty were still circulating throughout the country for Māori to sign.[\[44\]](#cite_note-50) With the signing of the Treaty and declaration of sovereignty the number of immigrants, particularly from the United Kingdom, began to increase.[\[45\]](#cite_note-51)

[![Black and white engraving depicting a crowd of people](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay%2C_New_Zealand.jpg/290px-1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay%2C_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay,_New_Zealand.jpg)

New Zealand, still part of the colony of New South Wales, became a separate [Colony](http://fakehost/wiki/Crown_colony%20%22Crown%20colony%22) [of New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand%20%22Colony%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) on 1 July 1841.[\[46\]](#cite_note-52) Armed conflict began between the Colonial government and Māori in 1843 with the [Wairau Affray](http://fakehost/wiki/Wairau_Affray%20%22Wairau%20Affray%22) over land and disagreements over sovereignty. These conflicts, mainly in the North Island, saw thousands of Imperial troops and the Royal Navy come to New Zealand and became known as the [New Zealand Wars](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars%20%22New%20Zealand%20Wars%22). Following these armed conflicts, large amounts of [Māori land was confiscated by the government](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_land_confiscations%20%22New%20Zealand%20land%20confiscations%22) to meet settler demands.[\[47\]](#cite_note-53)

The colony gained a [representative government in 1852](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Constitution_Act_1852%20%22New%20Zealand%20Constitution%20Act%201852%22) and the [first Parliament](http://fakehost/wiki/1st_New_Zealand_Parliament%20%221st%20New%20Zealand%20Parliament%22) met in 1854.[\[48\]](#cite_note-G_and_N-54) In 1856 the colony effectively became self-governing, gaining responsibility over all domestic matters other than [native](http://fakehost/wiki/Indigenous_peoples%20%22Indigenous%20peoples%22) policy.[\[48\]](#cite_note-G_and_N-54) (Control over native policy was granted in the mid-1860s.[\[48\]](#cite_note-G_and_N-54)) Following concerns that the South Island might form a separate colony, premier [Alfred Domett](http://fakehost/wiki/Alfred_Domett%20%22Alfred%20Domett%22) moved a resolution to transfer the [capital](http://fakehost/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand%20%22Capital%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) from Auckland to a locality near [Cook Strait](http://fakehost/wiki/Cook_Strait%20%22Cook%20Strait%22).[\[49\]](#cite_note-55) Wellington was chosen for its central location, with Parliament officially sitting there for the first time in 1865.[\[50\]](#cite_note-56)

In 1891 the [Liberal Party](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Liberal_Party%20%22New%20Zealand%20Liberal%20Party%22) came to power as the first organised political party.[\[51\]](#cite_note-Liberal-57) The [Liberal Government](http://fakehost/wiki/Liberal_Government_of_New_Zealand%20%22Liberal%20Government%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), led by [Richard Seddon](http://fakehost/wiki/Richard_Seddon%20%22Richard%20Seddon%22) for most of its period in office,[\[52\]](#cite_note-58) passed many important social and economic measures. In 1893 New Zealand was the first nation in the world to grant all [women the right to vote](http://fakehost/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_New_Zealand%20%22Women's%20suffrage%20in%20New%20Zealand%22)
[\[51\]](#cite_note-Liberal-57) and in 1894 pioneered the [adoption of compulsory arbitration between employers and unions](http://fakehost/wiki/Industrial_Conciliation_and_Arbitration_Act_1894%20%22Industrial%20Conciliation%20and%20Arbitration%20Act%201894%22).[\[53\]](#cite_note-59)

In 1907, at the request of the New Zealand Parliament, King [Edward VII](http://fakehost/wiki/Edward_VII%20%22Edward%20VII%22) proclaimed New Zealand a [Dominion](http://fakehost/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand%20%22Dominion%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) within the British Empire,[\[54\]](#cite_note-60) reflecting its self-governing status.[\[55\]](#cite_note-61) In 1947 the country [adopted](http://fakehost/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1947%20%22Statute%20of%20Westminster%20Adoption%20Act%201947%22) the [Statute of Westminster](http://fakehost/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931%20%22Statute%20of%20Westminster%201931%22), confirming that the British Parliament could no longer legislate for New Zealand without the consent of New Zealand.[\[48\]](#cite_note-G_and_N-54)

Early in the 20th century, New Zealand was involved in world affairs, fighting in the [First](http://fakehost/wiki/Military_history_of_New_Zealand_in_World_War_I%20%22Military%20history%20of%20New%20Zealand%20in%20World%20War%20I%22) and [Second World Wars](http://fakehost/wiki/Military_history_of_New_Zealand_during_World_War_II%20%22Military%20history%20of%20New%20Zealand%20during%20World%20War%20II%22)
[\[56\]](#cite_note-62) and suffering through the [Great Depression](http://fakehost/wiki/Great_Depression%20%22Great%20Depression%22).[\[57\]](#cite_note-63) The depression led to the election of the [First Labour Government](http://fakehost/wiki/First_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand%20%22First%20Labour%20Government%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) and the establishment of a comprehensive [welfare state](http://fakehost/wiki/Welfare_state%20%22Welfare%20state%22) and a [protectionist](http://fakehost/wiki/Protectionist%20%22Protectionist%22) economy.[\[58\]](#cite_note-64) New Zealand experienced increasing prosperity following the Second World War[\[59\]](#cite_note-65) and Māori began to leave their traditional rural life and move to the cities in search of work.[\[60\]](#cite_note-66) A [Māori protest movement](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_protest_movement%20%22M%C4%81ori%20protest%20movement%22) developed, which criticised [Eurocentrism](http://fakehost/wiki/Eurocentrism%20%22Eurocentrism%22) and worked for greater recognition of [Māori culture](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture%20%22M%C4%81ori%20culture%22) and of the Treaty of Waitangi.[\[61\]](#cite_note-67) In 1975, a [Waitangi Tribunal](http://fakehost/wiki/Waitangi_Tribunal%20%22Waitangi%20Tribunal%22) was set up to investigate alleged breaches of the Treaty, and it was enabled to investigate historic grievances in 1985.[\[41\]](#cite_note-Wilson2009-47) The government has negotiated [settlements of these grievances](http://fakehost/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_claims_and_settlements%20%22Treaty%20of%20Waitangi%20claims%20and%20settlements%22) with many iwi,[\[62\]](#cite_note-68) although [Māori claims to the foreshore and seabed](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy%20%22New%20Zealand%20foreshore%20and%20seabed%20controversy%22) have proved controversial in the 2000s.[\[63\]](#cite_note-69)
[\[64\]](#cite_note-70)

Government and politics
-----------------------

[![The Queen wearing her New Zealand insignia](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Queen_Elizabeth_II_of_New_Zealand_2.jpg/152px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_of_New_Zealand_2.jpg)](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")

[![A smiling woman wearing a black dress](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Ardern_Cropped.png/152px-Ardern_Cropped.png)](/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern "Jacinda Ardern")

New Zealand is a [constitutional monarchy](http://fakehost/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy%20%22Constitutional%20monarchy%22) with a [parliamentary democracy](http://fakehost/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy%20%22Parliamentary%20democracy%22),[\[65\]](#cite_note-GG_constitution-71) although [its constitution](http://fakehost/wiki/Constitution_of_New_Zealand%20%22Constitution%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) is [not codified](http://fakehost/wiki/Uncodified_constitution%20%22Uncodified%20constitution%22).[\[66\]](#cite_note-Economist_factsheet-72) [Elizabeth II](http://fakehost/wiki/Elizabeth_II%20%22Elizabeth%20II%22) is the [Queen of New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/Queen_of_New_Zealand%20%22Queen%20of%20New%20Zealand%22)
[\[67\]](#cite_note-73) and thus the [head of state](http://fakehost/wiki/Head_of_state%20%22Head%20of%20state%22).[\[68\]](#cite_note-74) The Queen is represented by the [governor-general](http://fakehost/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand%20%22Governor-General%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), whom she appoints on the [advice](http://fakehost/wiki/Advice_/(constitutional/) "Advice (constitutional)") of the [prime minister](http://fakehost/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand%20%22Prime%20Minister%20of%20New%20Zealand%22).[\[69\]](#cite_note-75) The governor-general can exercise the Crown's [prerogative powers](http://fakehost/wiki/Prerogative_powers%20%22Prerogative%20powers%22), such as reviewing cases of injustice and making appointments of [ministers](http://fakehost/wiki/Ministers_of_the_New_Zealand_Government%20%22Ministers%20of%20the%20New%20Zealand%20Government%22), ambassadors and other key public officials,[\[70\]](#cite_note-76) and in rare situations, the [reserve powers](http://fakehost/wiki/Reserve_power%20%22Reserve%20power%22) (e.g. the power to dissolve parliament or refuse the [royal assent](http://fakehost/wiki/Royal_assent%20%22Royal%20assent%22) of a [bill](http://fakehost/wiki/Bill_/(law/) "Bill (law)") into law).[\[71\]](#cite_note-reserve_Powers-77) The powers of the monarch and the governor-general are limited by constitutional constraints and they cannot normally be exercised without the advice of ministers.[\[71\]](#cite_note-reserve_Powers-77)

The [New Zealand Parliament](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament%20%22New%20Zealand%20Parliament%22) holds [legislative power](http://fakehost/wiki/Legislative_power%20%22Legislative%20power%22) and consists of the Queen and the [House of Representatives](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives%20%22New%20Zealand%20House%20of%20Representatives%22).[\[72\]](#cite_note-parliament_facts-78) It also included an upper house, the [Legislative Council](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Legislative_Council%20%22New%20Zealand%20Legislative%20Council%22), until this was abolished in 1950.[\[72\]](#cite_note-parliament_facts-78) The [supremacy of parliament](http://fakehost/wiki/Supremacy_of_parliament%20%22Supremacy%20of%20parliament%22) over the Crown and other government institutions was established in England by the [Bill of Rights 1689](http://fakehost/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689%20%22Bill%20of%20Rights%201689%22) and has been ratified as law in New Zealand.[\[72\]](#cite_note-parliament_facts-78) The House of Representatives is democratically elected and a government is formed from the party or [coalition](http://fakehost/wiki/Coalition_government%20%22Coalition%20government%22) with the majority of seats. If no majority is formed, a [minority government](http://fakehost/wiki/Minority_government%20%22Minority%20government%22) can be formed if support from other parties during [confidence and supply](http://fakehost/wiki/Confidence_and_supply%20%22Confidence%20and%20supply%22) votes is assured.[\[72\]](#cite_note-parliament_facts-78) The governor-general appoints ministers under advice from the prime minister, who is by [convention](http://fakehost/wiki/Constitutional_convention_/(political_custom/) "Constitutional convention (political custom)") the [parliamentary leader](http://fakehost/wiki/Parliamentary_leader%20%22Parliamentary%20leader%22) of the governing party or coalition.[\[73\]](#cite_note-79) [Cabinet](http://fakehost/wiki/Cabinet_of_New_Zealand%20%22Cabinet%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), formed by ministers and led by the prime minister, is the highest policy-making body in government and responsible for deciding significant government actions.[\[74\]](#cite_note-80) Members of Cabinet make major decisions collectively, and are therefore [collectively responsible](http://fakehost/wiki/Cabinet_collective_responsibility%20%22Cabinet%20collective%20responsibility%22) for the consequences of these decisions.[\[75\]](#cite_note-81)

A [parliamentary general election](http://fakehost/wiki/Elections_in_New_Zealand%20%22Elections%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) must be called no later than three years after the previous election.[\[76\]](#cite_note-82) Almost all general elections between [1853](http://fakehost/wiki/1853_New_Zealand_general_election%20%221853%20New%20Zealand%20general%20election%22) and [1993](http://fakehost/wiki/1993_New_Zealand_general_election%20%221993%20New%20Zealand%20general%20election%22) were held under the [first-past-the-post voting](http://fakehost/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting%20%22First-past-the-post%20voting%22) system.[\[77\]](#cite_note-road-83) Since the [1996 election](http://fakehost/wiki/1996_New_Zealand_general_election%20%221996%20New%20Zealand%20general%20election%22), a form of [proportional representation](http://fakehost/wiki/Proportional_representation%20%22Proportional%20representation%22) called [mixed-member proportional](http://fakehost/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional%20%22Mixed-member%20proportional%22) (MMP) has been used.[\[66\]](#cite_note-Economist_factsheet-72) Under the MMP system, each person has two votes; one is for a candidate standing in the voter's [electorate](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_electorates%20%22New%20Zealand%20electorates%22) and the other is for a party. Since the [2014 election](http://fakehost/wiki/2014_New_Zealand_general_election%20%222014%20New%20Zealand%20general%20election%22), there have been 71 electorates (which include seven [Māori electorates](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_electorates%20%22M%C4%81ori%20electorates%22) in which only Māori can optionally vote),[\[78\]](#cite_note-84) and the remaining 49 of the 120 seats are assigned so that representation in parliament reflects the party vote, with the threshold that a party must win at least one electorate or 5% of the total party vote before it is eligible for a seat.[\[79\]](#cite_note-85)

[![A block of buildings fronted by a large statue.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Seddon_Statue_in_Parliament_Grounds.jpg/220px-Seddon_Statue_in_Parliament_Grounds.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seddon_Statue_in_Parliament_Grounds.jpg)

Elections since the 1930s have been dominated by two political parties, [National](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party%20%22New%20Zealand%20National%20Party%22) and [Labour](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party%20%22New%20Zealand%20Labour%20Party%22).[\[77\]](#cite_note-road-83) Between March 2005 and August 2006, New Zealand became the first country in the world in which all the highest offices in the land—head of state, governor-general, prime minister, [speaker](http://fakehost/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives%20%22Speaker%20of%20the%20New%20Zealand%20House%20of%20Representatives%22) and [chief justice](http://fakehost/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_Zealand%20%22Chief%20Justice%20of%20New%20Zealand%22)—were occupied simultaneously by women.[\[80\]](#cite_note-86) The current prime minister is [Jacinda Ardern](http://fakehost/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern%20%22Jacinda%20Ardern%22), who has been in office since 26 October 2017.[\[81\]](#cite_note-87) She is the country's third female prime minister.[\[82\]](#cite_note-88)

[New Zealand's judiciary](http://fakehost/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_Zealand%20%22Judiciary%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), headed by the chief justice,[\[83\]](#cite_note-89) includes the [Supreme Court](http://fakehost/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_Zealand%20%22Supreme%20Court%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), [Court of Appeal](http://fakehost/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_New_Zealand%20%22Court%20of%20Appeal%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), the [High Court](http://fakehost/wiki/High_Court_of_New_Zealand%20%22High%20Court%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), and subordinate courts.[\[84\]](#cite_note-90) Judges and judicial officers are appointed non-politically and under strict rules regarding tenure to help maintain [judicial independence](http://fakehost/wiki/Judicial_independence%20%22Judicial%20independence%22).[\[66\]](#cite_note-Economist_factsheet-72) This theoretically allows the judiciary to interpret the law based solely on the legislation enacted by Parliament without other influences on their decisions.[\[85\]](#cite_note-91)

New Zealand is identified as one of the world's most stable and well-governed states.[\[86\]](#cite_note-92) As at 2017, the country was ranked fourth in the strength of its democratic institutions,[\[87\]](#cite_note-93) and first in government transparency and [lack of corruption](http://fakehost/wiki/Corruption_in_New_Zealand%20%22Corruption%20in%20New%20Zealand%22).[\[88\]](#cite_note-94) A 2017 [Human Rights Report](http://fakehost/wiki/Country_Reports_on_Human_Rights_Practices%20%22Country%20Reports%20on%20Human%20Rights%20Practices%22) by the U.S. Department of State noted that the government generally [respected the rights](http://fakehost/wiki/Human_rights_in_New_Zealand%20%22Human%20rights%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) of individuals, but voiced concerns regarding the social status of the Māori population.[\[89\]](#cite_note-95) New Zealand ranks highly for civic participation in the political process, with 77% [voter turnout](http://fakehost/wiki/Voter_turnout%20%22Voter%20turnout%22) during recent elections, compared to an OECD average of 69%.[\[90\]](#cite_note-96)

### Foreign relations and military

[![A squad of men kneel in the desert sand while performing a war dance](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg/220px-E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg)

Early colonial New Zealand allowed the British Government to determine external trade and be responsible for foreign policy.[\[91\]](#cite_note-97) The 1923 and 1926 [Imperial Conferences](http://fakehost/wiki/Imperial_Conference%20%22Imperial%20Conference%22) decided that New Zealand should be allowed to negotiate its own political [treaties](http://fakehost/wiki/Treaty%20%22Treaty%22) and the first commercial treaty was ratified in 1928 with Japan. On 3 September 1939 New Zealand allied itself with Britain and [declared war](http://fakehost/wiki/Declaration_of_war%20%22Declaration%20of%20war%22) on Germany with Prime Minister [Michael Joseph Savage](http://fakehost/wiki/Michael_Joseph_Savage%20%22Michael%20Joseph%20Savage%22) proclaiming, "Where she goes, we go; where she stands, we stand."[\[92\]](#cite_note-98)

In 1951 the United Kingdom became increasingly focused on its European interests,[\[93\]](#cite_note-99) while New Zealand joined [Australia](http://fakehost/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93New_Zealand_relations%20%22Australia%E2%80%93New%20Zealand%20relations%22) and the [United States](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand%E2%80%93United_States_relations%20%22New%20Zealand%E2%80%93United%20States%20relations%22) in the [ANZUS](http://fakehost/wiki/ANZUS%20%22ANZUS%22) security treaty.[\[94\]](#cite_note-100) The influence of the United States on New Zealand weakened following protests over the [Vietnam War](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_in_the_Vietnam_War%20%22New%20Zealand%20in%20the%20Vietnam%20War%22),[\[95\]](#cite_note-101) the refusal of the United States to admonish France after the [sinking of the _Rainbow Warrior_](http://fakehost/wiki/Sinking_of_the_Rainbow_Warrior%20%22Sinking%20of%20the%20Rainbow%20Warrior%22),[\[96\]](#cite_note-102) disagreements over environmental and agricultural trade issues and [New Zealand's nuclear-free policy](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_nuclear-free_zone%20%22New%20Zealand%20nuclear-free%20zone%22).[\[97\]](#cite_note-103)
[\[98\]](#cite_note-104) Despite the United States' suspension of ANZUS obligations the treaty remained in effect between New Zealand and Australia, whose foreign policy has followed a similar historical trend.[\[99\]](#cite_note-105) Close political contact is maintained between the two countries, with [free trade agreements](http://fakehost/wiki/Closer_Economic_Relations%20%22Closer%20Economic%20Relations%22) and [travel arrangements](http://fakehost/wiki/Trans-Tasman_Travel_Arrangement%20%22Trans-Tasman%20Travel%20Arrangement%22) that allow citizens to visit, live and work in both countries without restrictions.[\[100\]](#cite_note-NZ_in_brief-106) In 2013 there were about 650,000 New Zealand citizens living in Australia, which is equivalent to 15% of the resident population of New Zealand.[\[101\]](#cite_note-107)

[![A soldier in a green army uniform faces forwards](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/ANZAC_Day_service_at_the_National_War_Memorial_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force_%2820%29.jpg/220px-ANZAC_Day_service_at_the_National_War_Memorial_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force_%2820%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ANZAC_Day_service_at_the_National_War_Memorial_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force_\(20\).jpg)

[Anzac Day](http://fakehost/wiki/Anzac_Day%20%22Anzac%20Day%22) service at the National War Memorial

New Zealand has a strong presence among the [Pacific Island](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Island%20%22Pacific%20Island%22) countries. A large proportion of New Zealand's aid goes to these countries and many Pacific people migrate to New Zealand for employment.[\[102\]](#cite_note-108) Permanent migration is regulated under the 1970 Samoan Quota Scheme and the 2002 Pacific Access Category, which allow up to 1,100 Samoan nationals and up to 750 other Pacific Islanders respectively to become permanent New Zealand residents each year. A seasonal workers scheme for temporary migration was introduced in 2007 and in 2009 about 8,000 Pacific Islanders were employed under it.[\[103\]](#cite_note-109) A [regional power](http://fakehost/wiki/Regional_power%20%22Regional%20power%22),[\[104\]](#cite_note-110) New Zealand is involved in the [Pacific Islands Forum](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Forum%20%22Pacific%20Islands%20Forum%22), the [Pacific Community](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Community%20%22Pacific%20Community%22), [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](http://fakehost/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation%20%22Asia-Pacific%20Economic%20Cooperation%22) and the [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](http://fakehost/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations%20%22Association%20of%20Southeast%20Asian%20Nations%22) Regional Forum (including the [East Asia Summit](http://fakehost/wiki/East_Asia_Summit%20%22East%20Asia%20Summit%22)).[\[100\]](#cite_note-NZ_in_brief-106) New Zealand is a member of the [United Nations](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_and_the_United_Nations%20%22New%20Zealand%20and%20the%20United%20Nations%22),[\[105\]](#cite_note-111) the [Commonwealth of Nations](http://fakehost/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations%20%22Commonwealth%20of%20Nations%22)
[\[106\]](#cite_note-112) and the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](http://fakehost/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development%20%22Organisation%20for%20Economic%20Co-operation%20and%20Development%22) (OECD),[\[107\]](#cite_note-113) and participates in the [Five Power Defence Arrangements](http://fakehost/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements%20%22Five%20Power%20Defence%20Arrangements%22).[\[108\]](#cite_note-114)

New Zealand's military services—the Defence Force—comprise the [New Zealand Army](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Army%20%22New%20Zealand%20Army%22), the [Royal New Zealand Air Force](http://fakehost/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Air_Force%20%22Royal%20New%20Zealand%20Air%20Force%22) and the [Royal New Zealand Navy](http://fakehost/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Navy%20%22Royal%20New%20Zealand%20Navy%22).[\[109\]](#cite_note-115) New Zealand's [national defence](http://fakehost/wiki/National_security%20%22National%20security%22) needs are modest, since a direct attack is unlikely.[\[110\]](#cite_note-116) However, its military has [had a global presence](http://fakehost/wiki/Military_history_of_New_Zealand%20%22Military%20history%20of%20New%20Zealand%22). The country fought in both world wars, with notable campaigns in [Gallipoli](http://fakehost/wiki/Gallipoli_Campaign%20%22Gallipoli%20Campaign%22), [Crete](http://fakehost/wiki/Battle_of_Crete%20%22Battle%20of%20Crete%22),[\[111\]](#cite_note-117) [El Alamein](http://fakehost/wiki/Second_Battle_of_El_Alamein%20%22Second%20Battle%20of%20El%20Alamein%22)
[\[112\]](#cite_note-118) and [Cassino](http://fakehost/wiki/Battle_of_Monte_Cassino%20%22Battle%20of%20Monte%20Cassino%22).[\[113\]](#cite_note-119) The Gallipoli campaign played an important part in fostering New Zealand's [national identity](http://fakehost/wiki/National_identity%20%22National%20identity%22)
[\[114\]](#cite_note-120)
[\[115\]](#cite_note-121) and strengthened the [ANZAC](http://fakehost/wiki/Australian_and_New_Zealand_Army_Corps%20%22Australian%20and%20New%20Zealand%20Army%20Corps%22) tradition it shares with Australia.[\[116\]](#cite_note-122)

In addition to Vietnam and the two world wars, New Zealand fought in the [Second Boer War](http://fakehost/wiki/Second_Boer_War%20%22Second%20Boer%20War%22),[\[117\]](#cite_note-123) the [Korean War](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_in_the_Korean_War%20%22New%20Zealand%20in%20the%20Korean%20War%22),[\[118\]](#cite_note-124) the [Malayan Emergency](http://fakehost/wiki/Malayan_Emergency%20%22Malayan%20Emergency%22),[\[119\]](#cite_note-125) the [Gulf War](http://fakehost/wiki/Gulf_War%20%22Gulf%20War%22) and the [Afghanistan War](http://fakehost/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_/(2001%E2%80%93present/) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)"). It has contributed forces to several regional and global peacekeeping missions, such as those in [Cyprus](http://fakehost/wiki/Cyprus_dispute%20%22Cyprus%20dispute%22), [Somalia](http://fakehost/wiki/Somali_Civil_War%20%22Somali%20Civil%20War%22), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](http://fakehost/wiki/Bosnian_War%20%22Bosnian%20War%22), the [Sinai](http://fakehost/wiki/Suez_Crisis%20%22Suez%20Crisis%22), [Angola](http://fakehost/wiki/Angolan_Civil_War%20%22Angolan%20Civil%20War%22), [Cambodia](http://fakehost/wiki/Cambodian%E2%80%93Vietnamese_War%20%22Cambodian%E2%80%93Vietnamese%20War%22), the [Iran–Iraq](http://fakehost/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War%20%22Iran%E2%80%93Iraq%20War%22) border, [Bougainville](http://fakehost/wiki/Bougainville_Campaign%20%22Bougainville%20Campaign%22), [East Timor](http://fakehost/wiki/Operation_Astute%20%22Operation%20Astute%22), and the [Solomon Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Solomon_Islands#Civil_war%20%22Solomon%20Islands%22).[\[120\]](#cite_note-126)

### Local government and external territories

[![Map with the North, South, Stewart/Rakiura, Tokelau, Cook, Niue, Kermadec, Chatham, Bounty, Antipodes, Snare, Auckland and Campbell Islands highlighted. New Zealand's segment of Antarctica (the Ross Dependency) is also highlighted.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/NZL_orthographic_NaturalEarth_labelled_en.svg/400px-NZL_orthographic_NaturalEarth_labelled_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NZL_orthographic_NaturalEarth_labelled_en.svg)

The early European settlers divided New Zealand into [provinces](http://fakehost/wiki/Provinces_of_New_Zealand%20%22Provinces%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), which had a degree of autonomy.[\[121\]](#cite_note-nine_provinces-127) Because of financial pressures and the desire to consolidate railways, education, land sales and other policies, government was centralised and the provinces were abolished in 1876.[\[122\]](#cite_note-128) The provinces are remembered in [regional public holidays](http://fakehost/wiki/Public_holidays_in_New_Zealand%20%22Public%20holidays%20in%20New%20Zealand%22)
[\[123\]](#cite_note-129) and sporting rivalries.[\[124\]](#cite_note-130)

Since 1876, various councils have administered local areas under legislation determined by the central government.[\[121\]](#cite_note-nine_provinces-127)
[\[125\]](#cite_note-131) In 1989, the government reorganised local government into the current two-tier structure of [regional councils](http://fakehost/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand%20%22Regions%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) and [territorial authorities](http://fakehost/wiki/Territorial_authorities%20%22Territorial%20authorities%22).[\[126\]](#cite_note-Sancton2000-132) The [249 municipalities](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_former_territorial_authorities_in_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20former%20territorial%20authorities%20in%20New%20Zealand%22)
[\[126\]](#cite_note-Sancton2000-132) that existed in 1975 have now been consolidated into 67 territorial authorities and 11 regional councils.[\[127\]](#cite_note-133) The regional councils' role is to regulate "the natural environment with particular emphasis on [resource management](http://fakehost/wiki/Resource_Management_Act_1991%20%22Resource%20Management%20Act%201991%22)",[\[126\]](#cite_note-Sancton2000-132) while territorial authorities are responsible for sewage, water, local roads, building consents and other local matters.[\[128\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmeltJui_Lin200933-134)
[\[129\]](#cite_note-glossary-135) Five of the territorial councils are [unitary authorities](http://fakehost/wiki/Unitary_authority%20%22Unitary%20authority%22) and also act as regional councils.[\[129\]](#cite_note-glossary-135) The territorial authorities consist of 13 city councils, 53 [district](http://fakehost/wiki/Districts_of_New_Zealand%20%22Districts%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) councils, and the [Chatham Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Chatham_Islands%20%22Chatham%20Islands%22) Council. While officially the Chatham Islands Council is not a unitary authority, it undertakes many functions of a regional council.[\[130\]](#cite_note-136)

The Realm of New Zealand, one of 16 [Commonwealth realms](http://fakehost/wiki/Commonwealth_realm%20%22Commonwealth%20realm%22),[\[131\]](#cite_note-137) is the entire area over which the Queen of New Zealand is [sovereign](http://fakehost/wiki/Sovereign%20%22Sovereign%22), and comprises New Zealand, [Tokelau](http://fakehost/wiki/Tokelau%20%22Tokelau%22), the [Ross Dependency](http://fakehost/wiki/Ross_Dependency%20%22Ross%20Dependency%22), the [Cook Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Cook_Islands%20%22Cook%20Islands%22) and [Niue](http://fakehost/wiki/Niue%20%22Niue%22).[\[65\]](#cite_note-GG_constitution-71) The Cook Islands and Niue are self-governing states in [free association](http://fakehost/wiki/Associated_state%20%22Associated%20state%22) with New Zealand.[\[132\]](#cite_note-138)
[\[133\]](#cite_note-139) The New Zealand Parliament cannot pass legislation for these countries, but with their consent can act on behalf of them in foreign affairs and defence. Tokelau is classified as a [non-self-governing territory](http://fakehost/wiki/United_Nations_list_of_Non-Self-Governing_Territories%20%22United%20Nations%20list%20of%20Non-Self-Governing%20Territories%22), but is administered by a council of three elders (one from each Tokelauan [atoll](http://fakehost/wiki/Atoll%20%22Atoll%22)).[\[134\]](#cite_note-140) The Ross Dependency is New Zealand's [territorial claim in Antarctica](http://fakehost/wiki/Territorial_claims_in_Antarctica%20%22Territorial%20claims%20in%20Antarctica%22), where it operates the [Scott Base](http://fakehost/wiki/Scott_Base%20%22Scott%20Base%22) research facility.[\[135\]](#cite_note-141) [New Zealand nationality law](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_nationality_law%20%22New%20Zealand%20nationality%20law%22) treats all parts of the realm equally, so most people born in New Zealand, the Cook Islands, Niue, Tokelau and the Ross Dependency are New Zealand citizens.[\[136\]](#cite_note-142)
[\[n 7\]](#cite_note-144)

Environment
-----------

### Geography

[![Islands of New Zealand as seen from satellite](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg/170px-New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg)

New Zealand is located near the centre of the [water hemisphere](http://fakehost/wiki/Water_hemisphere%20%22Water%20hemisphere%22) and is made up of two main islands and a number of [smaller islands](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_islands_of_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20islands%20of%20New%20Zealand%22). The two main islands (the [North Island](http://fakehost/wiki/North_Island%20%22North%20Island%22), or _Te Ika-a-Māui_, and the [South Island](http://fakehost/wiki/South_Island%20%22South%20Island%22), or _Te Waipounamu_) are separated by [Cook Strait](http://fakehost/wiki/Cook_Strait%20%22Cook%20Strait%22), 22 kilometres (14 mi) wide at its narrowest point.[\[138\]](#cite_note-145) Besides the North and South Islands, the five largest inhabited islands are [Stewart Island](http://fakehost/wiki/Stewart_Island%20%22Stewart%20Island%22) (across the [Foveaux Strait](http://fakehost/wiki/Foveaux_Strait%20%22Foveaux%20Strait%22)), [Chatham Island](http://fakehost/wiki/Chatham_Island%20%22Chatham%20Island%22), [Great Barrier Island](http://fakehost/wiki/Great_Barrier_Island%20%22Great%20Barrier%20Island%22) (in the [Hauraki Gulf](http://fakehost/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf%20%22Hauraki%20Gulf%22)),[\[139\]](#cite_note-146) [D'Urville Island](http://fakehost/wiki/D%27Urville_Island_/(New_Zealand/) "D'Urville Island (New Zealand)") (in the [Marlborough Sounds](http://fakehost/wiki/Marlborough_Sounds%20%22Marlborough%20Sounds%22))
[\[140\]](#cite_note-147) and [Waiheke Island](http://fakehost/wiki/Waiheke_Island%20%22Waiheke%20Island%22) (about 22 km (14 mi) from central Auckland).[\[141\]](#cite_note-148)

[![A large mountain with a lake in the foreground](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Mt_Cook%2C_NZ.jpg/220px-Mt_Cook%2C_NZ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mt_Cook,_NZ.jpg)

[![Snow-capped mountain range](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/New_Zealand_moutain_ranges.jpg/220px-New_Zealand_moutain_ranges.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Zealand_moutain_ranges.jpg)

The Southern Alps stretch for 500 kilometres down the South Island

New Zealand is long and narrow (over 1,600 kilometres (990 mi) along its north-north-east axis with a maximum width of 400 kilometres (250 mi)),[\[142\]](#cite_note-149) with about 15,000 km (9,300 mi) of coastline[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) and a total land area of 268,000 square kilometres (103,500 sq mi).[\[144\]](#cite_note-151) Because of its far-flung outlying islands and long coastline, the country has extensive marine resources. Its [exclusive economic zone](http://fakehost/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_New_Zealand%20%22Exclusive%20economic%20zone%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) is one of the largest in the world, covering more than 15 times its land area.[\[145\]](#cite_note-152)

The South Island is the largest landmass of New Zealand. It is divided along its length by the [Southern Alps](http://fakehost/wiki/Southern_Alps%20%22Southern%20Alps%22).[\[146\]](#cite_note-153) There are 18 peaks over 3,000 metres (9,800 ft), the highest of which is [Aoraki / Mount Cook](http://fakehost/wiki/Aoraki_/_Mount_Cook%20%22Aoraki%20/%20Mount%20Cook%22) at 3,754 metres (12,316 ft).[\[147\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarden200552-154) [Fiordland](http://fakehost/wiki/Fiordland%20%22Fiordland%22)'s steep mountains and deep [fiords](http://fakehost/wiki/Fiord%20%22Fiord%22) record the extensive ice age glaciation of this southwestern corner of the South Island.[\[148\]](#cite_note-155) The North Island is less mountainous but is [marked by volcanism](http://fakehost/wiki/Volcanology_of_New_Zealand%20%22Volcanology%20of%20New%20Zealand%22).[\[149\]](#cite_note-156) The highly active [Taupo Volcanic Zone](http://fakehost/wiki/Taupo_Volcanic_Zone%20%22Taupo%20Volcanic%20Zone%22) has formed a large [volcanic plateau](http://fakehost/wiki/North_Island_Volcanic_Plateau%20%22North%20Island%20Volcanic%20Plateau%22), punctuated by the North Island's highest mountain, [Mount Ruapehu](http://fakehost/wiki/Mount_Ruapehu%20%22Mount%20Ruapehu%22) (2,797 metres (9,177 ft)). The plateau also hosts the country's largest lake, [Lake Taupo](http://fakehost/wiki/Lake_Taupo%20%22Lake%20Taupo%22),[\[150\]](#cite_note-157) nestled in the [caldera](http://fakehost/wiki/Caldera%20%22Caldera%22) of one of the world's most active [supervolcanoes](http://fakehost/wiki/Supervolcano%20%22Supervolcano%22).[\[151\]](#cite_note-158)

The country owes its varied topography, and perhaps even its emergence above the waves, to the dynamic boundary it straddles between the [Pacific](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Plate%20%22Pacific%20Plate%22) and [Indo-Australian Plates](http://fakehost/wiki/Indo-Australian_Plate%20%22Indo-Australian%20Plate%22).[\[152\]](#cite_note-Keith_2009-159) New Zealand is part of [Zealandia](http://fakehost/wiki/Zealandia%20%22Zealandia%22), a [microcontinent](http://fakehost/wiki/Microcontinent%20%22Microcontinent%22) nearly half the size of Australia that gradually submerged after breaking away from the [Gondwanan](http://fakehost/wiki/Gondwana%20%22Gondwana%22) supercontinent.[\[153\]](#cite_note-160) About 25 million years ago, a shift in [plate tectonic](http://fakehost/wiki/Plate_tectonic%20%22Plate%20tectonic%22) movements began to [contort and crumple](http://fakehost/wiki/Kaikoura_Orogeny%20%22Kaikoura%20Orogeny%22) the region. This is now most evident in the Southern Alps, formed by [compression of the crust](http://fakehost/wiki/Continental_collision%20%22Continental%20collision%22) beside the [Alpine Fault](http://fakehost/wiki/Alpine_Fault%20%22Alpine%20Fault%22). Elsewhere the plate boundary involves the [subduction](http://fakehost/wiki/Subduction%20%22Subduction%22) of one plate under the other, producing the [Puysegur Trench](http://fakehost/wiki/Puysegur_Trench%20%22Puysegur%20Trench%22) to the south, the [Hikurangi Trench](http://fakehost/wiki/Hikurangi_Trench%20%22Hikurangi%20Trench%22) east of the North Island, and the [Kermadec](http://fakehost/wiki/Kermadec_Trench%20%22Kermadec%20Trench%22) and [Tonga Trenches](http://fakehost/wiki/Tonga_Trench%20%22Tonga%20Trench%22)
[\[154\]](#cite_note-161) further north.[\[152\]](#cite_note-Keith_2009-159)

New Zealand is part of a region known as [Australasia](http://fakehost/wiki/Australasia%20%22Australasia%22), together with Australia.[\[155\]](#cite_note-NZOD-162) It also forms the southwestern extremity of the geographic and ethnographic region called [Polynesia](http://fakehost/wiki/Polynesia%20%22Polynesia%22).[\[156\]](#cite_note-163) The term [Oceania](http://fakehost/wiki/Oceania%20%22Oceania%22) is often used to denote the wider region encompassing the [Australian continent](http://fakehost/wiki/Australia_/(continent/) "Australia (continent)"), New Zealand and various islands in the Pacific Ocean that are not included in the [seven-continent](http://fakehost/wiki/Continent%20%22Continent%22) model.[\[157\]](#cite_note-164)

*   [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Pencarrow_Head%2C_Wellington%2C_New_Zealand_from_Santa_Regina%2C_24_Feb._2007.jpg/269px-Pencarrow_Head%2C_Wellington%2C_New_Zealand_from_Santa_Regina%2C_24_Feb._2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pencarrow_Head,_Wellington,_New_Zealand_from_Santa_Regina,_24_Feb._2007.jpg)
    

### Climate

New Zealand's climate is predominantly temperate [maritime](http://fakehost/wiki/Oceanic_climate%20%22Oceanic%20climate%22) ([Köppen](http://fakehost/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification%20%22K%C3%B6ppen%20climate%20classification%22): Cfb), with mean annual temperatures ranging from 10 °C (50 °F) in the south to 16 °C (61 °F) in the north.[\[158\]](#cite_note-Mullan2009-165) Historical [maxima and minima](http://fakehost/wiki/Maxima_and_minima%20%22Maxima%20and%20minima%22) are 42.4 °C (108.32 °F) in [Rangiora](http://fakehost/wiki/Rangiora%20%22Rangiora%22), [Canterbury](http://fakehost/wiki/Canterbury,_New_Zealand%20%22Canterbury,%20New%20Zealand%22) and −25.6 °C (−14.08 °F) in [Ranfurly](http://fakehost/wiki/Ranfurly,_New_Zealand%20%22Ranfurly,%20New%20Zealand%22), [Otago](http://fakehost/wiki/Otago%20%22Otago%22).[\[159\]](#cite_note-niwa-166) Conditions vary sharply across regions from extremely wet on the [West Coast](http://fakehost/wiki/West_Coast,_New_Zealand%20%22West%20Coast,%20New%20Zealand%22) of the South Island to almost [semi-arid](http://fakehost/wiki/Semi-arid_climate%20%22Semi-arid%20climate%22) in [Central Otago](http://fakehost/wiki/Central_Otago%20%22Central%20Otago%22) and the [Mackenzie Basin](http://fakehost/wiki/Mackenzie_Basin%20%22Mackenzie%20Basin%22) of inland Canterbury and [subtropical](http://fakehost/wiki/Subtropical%20%22Subtropical%22) in [Northland](http://fakehost/wiki/Northland_Peninsula%20%22Northland%20Peninsula%22).[\[160\]](#cite_note-167) Of the seven largest cities, [Christchurch](http://fakehost/wiki/Christchurch%20%22Christchurch%22) is the driest, receiving on average only 640 millimetres (25 in) of rain per year and Wellington the wettest, receiving almost twice that amount.[\[161\]](#cite_note-168) Auckland, Wellington and Christchurch all receive a yearly average of more than 2,000 hours of sunshine. The southern and southwestern parts of the South Island have a cooler and cloudier climate, with around 1,400–1,600 hours; the northern and northeastern parts of the South Island are the sunniest areas of the country and receive about 2,400–2,500 hours.[\[162\]](#cite_note-169) The general snow season is early June until early October, though [cold snaps](http://fakehost/wiki/Cold_snap%20%22Cold%20snap%22) can occur outside this season.[\[163\]](#cite_note-170) Snowfall is common in the eastern and southern parts of the South Island and mountain areas across the country.[\[158\]](#cite_note-Mullan2009-165)

The table below lists climate normals for the warmest and coldest months in New Zealand's six largest cities. North Island cities are generally warmest in February. South Island cities are warmest in January.
