### Biodiversity

[![Kiwi amongst sticks](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/TeTuatahianui.jpg/170px-TeTuatahianui.jpg)](/wiki/File:TeTuatahianui.jpg)

The endemic flightless [kiwi](http://fakehost/wiki/Kiwi%20%22Kiwi%22) is a national icon.

New Zealand's [geographic isolation](http://fakehost/wiki/Geographic_isolation%20%22Geographic%20isolation%22) for 80 million years[\[165\]](#cite_note-172) and island [biogeography](http://fakehost/wiki/Biogeography%20%22Biogeography%22) has influenced evolution of the country's species of [animals](http://fakehost/wiki/Fauna%20%22Fauna%22), [fungi](http://fakehost/wiki/Fungus%20%22Fungus%22) and [plants](http://fakehost/wiki/Flora%20%22Flora%22). Physical isolation has caused biological isolation, resulting in a dynamic evolutionary ecology with examples of very distinctive plants and animals as well as populations of widespread species.[\[166\]](#cite_note-173)
[\[167\]](#cite_note-174) About 82% of New Zealand's indigenous [vascular plants](http://fakehost/wiki/Vascular_plant%20%22Vascular%20plant%22) are [endemic](http://fakehost/wiki/Endemism%20%22Endemism%22), covering 1,944 species across 65 [genera](http://fakehost/wiki/Genus%20%22Genus%22).[\[168\]](#cite_note-FAQ-175)
[\[169\]](#cite_note-NZPCN_2006-176) The number of fungi recorded from New Zealand, including lichen-forming species, is not known, nor is the proportion of those fungi which are endemic, but one estimate suggests there are about 2,300 species of lichen-forming fungi in New Zealand[\[168\]](#cite_note-FAQ-175) and 40% of these are endemic.[\[170\]](#cite_note-177) The two main types of forest are those dominated by broadleaf trees with emergent [podocarps](http://fakehost/wiki/Podocarp%20%22Podocarp%22), or by [southern beech](http://fakehost/wiki/Nothofagus%20%22Nothofagus%22) in cooler climates.[\[171\]](#cite_note-178) The remaining vegetation types consist of grasslands, the majority of which are [tussock](http://fakehost/wiki/Tussock_grasslands_of_New_Zealand%20%22Tussock%20grasslands%20of%20New%20Zealand%22).[\[172\]](#cite_note-179)

Before the arrival of humans, an estimated 80% of the land was covered in forest, with only [high alpine](http://fakehost/wiki/Tree_line%20%22Tree%20line%22), wet, infertile and volcanic areas without trees.[\[173\]](#cite_note-180) Massive [deforestation](http://fakehost/wiki/Deforestation_in_New_Zealand%20%22Deforestation%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) occurred after humans arrived, with around half the forest cover lost to fire after Polynesian settlement.[\[174\]](#cite_note-181) Much of the remaining forest fell after European settlement, being logged or cleared to make room for pastoral farming, leaving forest occupying only 23% of the land.[\[175\]](#cite_note-182)

[![An artist's rendition of a Haast's eagle attacking two moa](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Giant_Haasts_eagle_attacking_New_Zealand_moa.jpg/220px-Giant_Haasts_eagle_attacking_New_Zealand_moa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giant_Haasts_eagle_attacking_New_Zealand_moa.jpg)

The giant [Haast's eagle](http://fakehost/wiki/Haast%27s_eagle%20%22Haast's%20eagle%22) died out when humans hunted its main prey, the [moa](http://fakehost/wiki/Moa%20%22Moa%22), to extinction.

The forests were dominated by [birds](http://fakehost/wiki/Birds_of_New_Zealand%20%22Birds%20of%20New%20Zealand%22), and the lack of mammalian predators led to some like the [kiwi](http://fakehost/wiki/Kiwi%20%22Kiwi%22), [kakapo](http://fakehost/wiki/Kakapo%20%22Kakapo%22), [weka](http://fakehost/wiki/Weka%20%22Weka%22) and [takahē](http://fakehost/wiki/Takah%C4%93%20%22Takah%C4%93%22) evolving [flightlessness](http://fakehost/wiki/Flightless_bird%20%22Flightless%20bird%22).[\[176\]](#cite_note-183) The arrival of humans, associated changes to habitat, and the introduction of [rats](http://fakehost/wiki/Polynesian_rat%20%22Polynesian%20rat%22), ferrets and other mammals led to the [extinction](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_animals_extinct_in_the_Holocene%20%22List%20of%20New%20Zealand%20animals%20extinct%20in%20the%20Holocene%22) of many bird species, including [large birds](http://fakehost/wiki/Megafauna%20%22Megafauna%22) like the [moa](http://fakehost/wiki/Moa%20%22Moa%22) and [Haast's eagle](http://fakehost/wiki/Haast%27s_eagle%20%22Haast's%20eagle%22).[\[177\]](#cite_note-Holdaway2009-184)
[\[178\]](#cite_note-185)

Other indigenous animals are represented by reptiles ([tuatara](http://fakehost/wiki/Tuatara%20%22Tuatara%22), [skinks](http://fakehost/wiki/Skink%20%22Skink%22) and [geckos](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_geckos_of_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20geckos%20of%20New%20Zealand%22)), [frogs](http://fakehost/wiki/Leiopelmatidae%20%22Leiopelmatidae%22),[\[179\]](#cite_note-186) [spiders](http://fakehost/wiki/Spiders_of_New_Zealand%20%22Spiders%20of%20New%20Zealand%22),[\[180\]](#cite_note-187) insects ([weta](http://fakehost/wiki/Weta%20%22Weta%22))
[\[181\]](#cite_note-188) and snails.[\[182\]](#cite_note-189) Some, such as the tuatara, are so unique that they have been called [living fossils](http://fakehost/wiki/Living_fossil%20%22Living%20fossil%22).[\[183\]](#cite_note-190) Three species of bats ([one](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_greater_short-tailed_bat%20%22New%20Zealand%20greater%20short-tailed%20bat%22) since extinct) were the only sign of native land mammals in New Zealand until the 2006 discovery of bones from [a unique, mouse-sized land mammal](http://fakehost/wiki/Saint_Bathans_mammal%20%22Saint%20Bathans%20mammal%22) at least 16 million years old.[\[184\]](#cite_note-191)
[\[185\]](#cite_note-192) Marine mammals however are abundant, with almost half the world's [cetaceans](http://fakehost/wiki/Cetacea%20%22Cetacea%22) (whales, dolphins, and [porpoises](http://fakehost/wiki/Porpoise%20%22Porpoise%22)) and large numbers of [fur seals](http://fakehost/wiki/Fur_seal%20%22Fur%20seal%22) reported in New Zealand waters.[\[186\]](#cite_note-193) Many seabirds breed in New Zealand, a third of them unique to the country.[\[187\]](#cite_note-194) More [penguin](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_birds_of_New_Zealand#Penguins%20%22List%20of%20birds%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) species are found in New Zealand than in any other country.[\[188\]](#cite_note-195)

Since human arrival, almost half of the country's vertebrate species have become extinct, including at least fifty-one birds, three frogs, three lizards, one freshwater fish, and one bat. Others are endangered or have had their range severely reduced.[\[177\]](#cite_note-Holdaway2009-184) However, New Zealand conservationists have pioneered several methods to help threatened wildlife recover, including island sanctuaries, pest control, wildlife translocation, fostering, and ecological [restoration of islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Island_restoration%20%22Island%20restoration%22) and [other selected areas](http://fakehost/wiki/Ecological_island%20%22Ecological%20island%22).[\[189\]](#cite_note-196)
[\[190\]](#cite_note-197)
[\[191\]](#cite_note-198)
[\[192\]](#cite_note-199)

Economy
-------

[![Boats docked in blue-green water. Plate glass skyscrapers rising up in the background.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Auckland_Waterfrt.jpg/220px-Auckland_Waterfrt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_Waterfrt.jpg)

New Zealand has an [advanced](http://fakehost/wiki/Advanced_economy%20%22Advanced%20economy%22) [market economy](http://fakehost/wiki/Market_economy%20%22Market%20economy%22),[\[193\]](#cite_note-200) ranked 16th in the 2018 [Human Development Index](http://fakehost/wiki/Human_Development_Index%20%22Human%20Development%20Index%22)
[\[8\]](#cite_note-HDI-12) and third in the 2018 [Index of Economic Freedom](http://fakehost/wiki/Index_of_Economic_Freedom%20%22Index%20of%20Economic%20Freedom%22).[\[194\]](#cite_note-201) It is a [high-income economy](http://fakehost/wiki/High-income_economy%20%22High-income%20economy%22) with a [nominal](http://fakehost/wiki/Nominal_value%20%22Nominal%20value%22) [gross domestic product](http://fakehost/wiki/Gross_domestic_product%20%22Gross%20domestic%20product%22) (GDP) per capita of [US$](http://fakehost/wiki/United_States_dollar%20%22United%20States%20dollar%22)36,254.[\[6\]](#cite_note-imf2-10) The currency is the [New Zealand dollar](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_dollar%20%22New%20Zealand%20dollar%22), informally known as the "Kiwi dollar"; it also circulates in the Cook Islands (see [Cook Islands dollar](http://fakehost/wiki/Cook_Islands_dollar%20%22Cook%20Islands%20dollar%22)), Niue, Tokelau, and the [Pitcairn Islands](http://fakehost/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands%20%22Pitcairn%20Islands%22).[\[195\]](#cite_note-202)

Historically, extractive industries have contributed strongly to New Zealand's economy, focussing at different times on sealing, whaling, [flax](http://fakehost/wiki/Phormium%20%22Phormium%22), gold, [kauri gum](http://fakehost/wiki/Kauri_gum%20%22Kauri%20gum%22), and native timber.[\[196\]](#cite_note-RWT_export_evolution-203) The first shipment of refrigerated meat on the _[Dunedin](http://fakehost/wiki/Dunedin_/(ship/) "Dunedin (ship)")_ in 1882 led to the establishment of meat and dairy exports to Britain, a trade which provided the basis for strong economic growth in New Zealand.[\[197\]](#cite_note-204) High demand for agricultural products from the United Kingdom and the United States helped New Zealanders achieve higher living standards than both Australia and Western Europe in the 1950s and 1960s.[\[198\]](#cite_note-205) In 1973, New Zealand's export market was reduced when the United Kingdom joined the [European Economic Community](http://fakehost/wiki/European_Economic_Community%20%22European%20Economic%20Community%22)
[\[199\]](#cite_note-206) and other compounding factors, such as the [1973 oil](http://fakehost/wiki/1973_oil_crisis%20%221973%20oil%20crisis%22) and [1979 energy](http://fakehost/wiki/1979_oil_crisis%20%221979%20oil%20crisis%22) crises, led to a severe [economic depression](http://fakehost/wiki/Depression_/(economics/) "Depression (economics)").[\[200\]](#cite_note-207) Living standards in New Zealand fell behind those of Australia and Western Europe, and by 1982 New Zealand had the lowest per-capita income of all the developed nations surveyed by [the World Bank](http://fakehost/wiki/World_Bank_Group%20%22World%20Bank%20Group%22).[\[201\]](#cite_note-208) In the mid-1980s New Zealand deregulated its [agricultural sector](http://fakehost/wiki/Agriculture_in_New_Zealand%20%22Agriculture%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) by phasing out [subsidies](http://fakehost/wiki/Agricultural_subsidy%20%22Agricultural%20subsidy%22) over a three-year period.[\[202\]](#cite_note-209)
[\[203\]](#cite_note-210) Since 1984, successive governments engaged in major [macroeconomic](http://fakehost/wiki/Macroeconomic%20%22Macroeconomic%22) restructuring (known first as [Rogernomics](http://fakehost/wiki/Rogernomics%20%22Rogernomics%22) and then [Ruthanasia](http://fakehost/wiki/Ruthanasia%20%22Ruthanasia%22)), rapidly transforming New Zealand from a [protected](http://fakehost/wiki/Protectionism%20%22Protectionism%22) and highly regulated economy to a liberalised [free-trade](http://fakehost/wiki/Free-trade%20%22Free-trade%22) economy.[\[204\]](#cite_note-Liberalisation-211)
[\[205\]](#cite_note-212)

[![Blue water against a backdrop of snow-capped mountains](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/MilfordSound.jpg/220px-MilfordSound.jpg)](/wiki/File:MilfordSound.jpg)

[Milford Sound](http://fakehost/wiki/Milford_Sound%20%22Milford%20Sound%22) is one of New Zealand's most famous tourist destinations.[\[206\]](#cite_note-213)

Unemployment peaked above 10% in 1991 and 1992,[\[207\]](#cite_note-unemployment-214) following the [1987 share market crash](http://fakehost/wiki/Black_Monday_/(1987/) "Black Monday (1987)"), but eventually fell to a record low (since 1986) of 3.7% in 2007 (ranking third from twenty-seven comparable OECD nations).[\[207\]](#cite_note-unemployment-214) However, the [global financial crisis](http://fakehost/wiki/Financial_crisis_of_2007%E2%80%932008%20%22Financial%20crisis%20of%202007%E2%80%932008%22) that followed had a major impact on New Zealand, with the GDP shrinking for five consecutive quarters, the longest recession in over thirty years,[\[208\]](#cite_note-215)
[\[209\]](#cite_note-216) and unemployment rising back to 7% in late 2009.[\[210\]](#cite_note-217) Unemployment rates for different age groups follow similar trends, but are consistently higher among youth. In the December 2014 quarter, the general unemployment rate was around 5.8%, while the unemployment rate for youth aged 15 to 21 was 15.6%.[\[207\]](#cite_note-unemployment-214) New Zealand has experienced a series of "[brain drains](http://fakehost/wiki/Brain_drain%20%22Brain%20drain%22)" since the 1970s[\[211\]](#cite_note-218) that still continue today.[\[212\]](#cite_note-219) Nearly one quarter of highly skilled workers live overseas, mostly in Australia and Britain, which is the largest proportion from any developed nation.[\[213\]](#cite_note-220) In recent decades, however, a "brain gain" has brought in educated professionals from Europe and less developed countries.[\[214\]](#cite_note-221)
[\[215\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBain200644-222) Today New Zealand's economy benefits from a high level of [innovation](http://fakehost/wiki/Innovation%20%22Innovation%22).[\[216\]](#cite_note-223)

### Trade

New Zealand is heavily dependent on international trade,[\[217\]](#cite_note-224) particularly in agricultural products.[\[218\]](#cite_note-225) Exports account for 24% of its output,[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) making New Zealand vulnerable to international commodity prices and global [economic slowdowns](http://fakehost/wiki/Recession%20%22Recession%22). Food products made up 55% of the value of all the country's exports in 2014; wood was the second largest earner (7%).[\[219\]](#cite_note-226) New Zealand's main trading partners, as at June 2018, are China ([NZ$](http://fakehost/wiki/NZ$%20%22NZ$%22)27.8b), Australia ($26.2b), the [European Union](http://fakehost/wiki/European_Union%20%22European%20Union%22) ($22.9b), the United States ($17.6b), and Japan ($8.4b).[\[220\]](#cite_note-GSTC_StatNZ-227) On 7 April 2008, New Zealand and China signed the [New Zealand–China Free Trade Agreement](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand%E2%80%93China_Free_Trade_Agreement%20%22New%20Zealand%E2%80%93China%20Free%20Trade%20Agreement%22), the first such agreement China has signed with a developed country.[\[221\]](#cite_note-228) The service sector is the largest sector in the economy, followed by manufacturing and construction and then farming and raw material extraction.[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) [Tourism](http://fakehost/wiki/Tourism_in_New_Zealand%20%22Tourism%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) plays a significant role in the economy, contributing $12.9 billion (or 5.6%) to New Zealand's total GDP and supporting 7.5% of the total workforce in 2016.[\[222\]](#cite_note-TourismStats-229) International visitor arrivals are expected to increase at a rate of 5.4% annually up to 2022.[\[222\]](#cite_note-TourismStats-229)

[![A Romney ewe with her two lambs](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Fauna_de_Nueva_Zelanda07.JPG/220px-Fauna_de_Nueva_Zelanda07.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fauna_de_Nueva_Zelanda07.JPG)

Wool has historically been one of New Zealand's major exports.

Wool was New Zealand's major agricultural export during the late 19th century.[\[196\]](#cite_note-RWT_export_evolution-203) Even as late as the 1960s it made up over a third of all export revenues,[\[196\]](#cite_note-RWT_export_evolution-203) but since then its price has steadily dropped relative to other commodities[\[223\]](#cite_note-230) and wool is no longer profitable for many farmers.[\[224\]](#cite_note-231) In contrast [dairy farming](http://fakehost/wiki/Dairy_farming_in_New_Zealand%20%22Dairy%20farming%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) increased, with the number of dairy cows doubling between 1990 and 2007,[\[225\]](#cite_note-232) to become New Zealand's largest export earner.[\[226\]](#cite_note-233) In the year to June 2018, dairy products accounted for 17.7% ($14.1 billion) of total exports,[\[220\]](#cite_note-GSTC_StatNZ-227) and the country's largest company, [Fonterra](http://fakehost/wiki/Fonterra%20%22Fonterra%22), controls almost one-third of the international dairy trade.[\[227\]](#cite_note-234) Other exports in 2017-18 were meat (8.8%), wood and wood products (6.2%), fruit (3.6%), machinery (2.2%) and wine (2.1%).[\[220\]](#cite_note-GSTC_StatNZ-227) [New Zealand's wine](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_wine%20%22New%20Zealand%20wine%22) industry has followed a similar trend to dairy, the number of vineyards doubling over the same period,[\[228\]](#cite_note-235) overtaking wool exports for the first time in 2007.[\[229\]](#cite_note-236)
[\[230\]](#cite_note-237)

### Infrastructure

[![A mid-size jet airliner in flight. The plane livery is all-black and features a New Zealand silver fern mark.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Air_New_Zealand%2C_Boeing_787-9_ZK-NZE_%27All_Blacks%27_NRT_%2827091961041%29.jpg/220px-Air_New_Zealand%2C_Boeing_787-9_ZK-NZE_%27All_Blacks%27_NRT_%2827091961041%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Air_New_Zealand,_Boeing_787-9_ZK-NZE_%27All_Blacks%27_NRT_\(27091961041\).jpg)

In 2015, [renewable energy](http://fakehost/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_New_Zealand%20%22Renewable%20energy%20in%20New%20Zealand%22), primarily [geothermal](http://fakehost/wiki/Geothermal_power_in_New_Zealand%20%22Geothermal%20power%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) and [hydroelectric power](http://fakehost/wiki/Hydroelectric_power_in_New_Zealand%20%22Hydroelectric%20power%20in%20New%20Zealand%22), generated 40.1% of [New Zealand's gross energy](http://fakehost/wiki/Energy_in_New_Zealand%20%22Energy%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) supply.[\[231\]](#cite_note-Energy2015-238) Geothermal power alone accounted for 22% of New Zealand's energy in 2015.[\[231\]](#cite_note-Energy2015-238)

The provision of [water supply and sanitation](http://fakehost/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_New_Zealand%20%22Water%20supply%20and%20sanitation%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) is generally of good quality. Regional authorities provide water abstraction, treatment and distribution infrastructure to most developed areas.[\[232\]](#cite_note-239)
[\[233\]](#cite_note-240)

[New Zealand's transport](http://fakehost/wiki/Transport_in_New_Zealand%20%22Transport%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) network comprises 94,000 kilometres (58,410 mi) of roads, including 199 kilometres (124 mi) of motorways,[\[234\]](#cite_note-241) and 4,128 kilometres (2,565 mi) of railway lines.[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) Most major cities and towns are linked by bus services, although the private car is the predominant mode of transport.[\[235\]](#cite_note-242) The [railways](http://fakehost/wiki/Rail_transport_in_New_Zealand%20%22Rail%20transport%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) were privatised in 1993, but were re-nationalised by the government in stages between 2004 and 2008. The state-owned enterprise [KiwiRail](http://fakehost/wiki/KiwiRail%20%22KiwiRail%22) now operates the railways, with the exception of commuter services in Auckland and Wellington which are operated by [Transdev](http://fakehost/wiki/Transdev_Auckland%20%22Transdev%20Auckland%22)
[\[236\]](#cite_note-243) and [Metlink](http://fakehost/wiki/Public_transport_in_the_Wellington_Region%20%22Public%20transport%20in%20the%20Wellington%20Region%22),[\[237\]](#cite_note-244) respectively. Railways run the length of the country, although most lines now carry freight rather than passengers.[\[238\]](#cite_note-245) Most international visitors arrive via air[\[239\]](#cite_note-246) and New Zealand has [six international airports](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_airports_in_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20airports%20in%20New%20Zealand%22), but currently only the [Auckland](http://fakehost/wiki/Auckland_Airport%20%22Auckland%20Airport%22) and [Christchurch airports](http://fakehost/wiki/Christchurch_International_Airport%20%22Christchurch%20International%20Airport%22) connect directly with countries other than Australia or Fiji.[\[240\]](#cite_note-247)

The [New Zealand Post Office](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Post_Office%20%22New%20Zealand%20Post%20Office%22) had a monopoly over [telecommunications in New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/Telecommunications_in_New_Zealand%20%22Telecommunications%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) until 1987 when [Telecom New Zealand](http://fakehost/wiki/Spark_New_Zealand%20%22Spark%20New%20Zealand%22) was formed, initially as a state-owned enterprise and then privatised in 1990.[\[241\]](#cite_note-Wilson2010-248) [Chorus](http://fakehost/wiki/Chorus_Limited%20%22Chorus%20Limited%22), which was split from Telecom (now Spark) in 2011,[\[242\]](#cite_note-249) still owns the majority of the telecommunications infrastructure, but competition from other providers has increased.[\[241\]](#cite_note-Wilson2010-248) A large-scale rollout of gigabit-capable [fibre to the premises](http://fakehost/wiki/Fibre_to_the_premises%20%22Fibre%20to%20the%20premises%22), branded as [Ultra-Fast Broadband](http://fakehost/wiki/Ultra-Fast_Broadband%20%22Ultra-Fast%20Broadband%22), began in 2009 with a target of being available to 87% of the population by 2022.[\[243\]](#cite_note-250) As of 2017, the United Nations [International Telecommunication Union](http://fakehost/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union%20%22International%20Telecommunication%20Union%22) ranks New Zealand 13th in the development of information and communications infrastructure.[\[244\]](#cite_note-251)

Demography
----------

[![Stationary population pyramid broken down into 21 age ranges.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/New_Zealandpop.svg/280px-New_Zealandpop.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_Zealandpop.svg)

The [2013 New Zealand census](http://fakehost/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census%20%222013%20New%20Zealand%20census%22) enumerated a resident population of 4,242,048, an increase of 5.3% over the 2006 figure.[\[245\]](#cite_note-252)
[\[n 8\]](#cite_note-254) As of September 2019, the total population has risen to an estimated 4,933,210.[\[5\]](#cite_note-populationestimate-9)

New Zealand is a predominantly urban country, with 73.0% of the population living in the seventeen main [urban areas](http://fakehost/wiki/Urban_area%20%22Urban%20area%22) (i.e. population 30,000 or greater) and 55.1% living in the four largest cities of [Auckland](http://fakehost/wiki/Auckland%20%22Auckland%22), [Christchurch](http://fakehost/wiki/Christchurch%20%22Christchurch%22), [Wellington](http://fakehost/wiki/Wellington%20%22Wellington%22), and [Hamilton](http://fakehost/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand%20%22Hamilton,%20New%20Zealand%22).[\[247\]](#cite_note-Urban-255) New Zealand cities generally rank highly on international livability measures. For instance, in 2016 Auckland was ranked the world's third [most liveable city](http://fakehost/wiki/Most_liveable_city%20%22Most%20liveable%20city%22) and Wellington the twelfth by the [Mercer Quality of Living Survey](http://fakehost/wiki/Mercer_Quality_of_Living_Survey%20%22Mercer%20Quality%20of%20Living%20Survey%22).[\[248\]](#cite_note-256)

Life expectancy for New Zealanders in 2012 was 84 years for females, and 80.2 years for males.[\[249\]](#cite_note-257) Life expectancy at birth is forecast to increase from 80 years to 85 years in 2050 and infant mortality is expected to decline.[\[250\]](#cite_note-UNtwspop-258) New Zealand's [fertility rate](http://fakehost/wiki/Fertility_rate%20%22Fertility%20rate%22) of 2.1 is relatively high for a developed country, and natural births account for a significant proportion of [population growth](http://fakehost/wiki/Replacement_fertility_rate%20%22Replacement%20fertility%20rate%22). Consequently, the country has a young population compared to most industrialised nations, with 20% of New Zealanders being 14 years old or younger.[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) In 2018 the [median age](http://fakehost/wiki/Median_age%20%22Median%20age%22) of the New Zealand population was 38.1 years.[\[251\]](#cite_note-259) By 2050 the median age is projected to rise to 43 years and the percentage of people 60 years of age and older to rise from 18% to 29%.[\[250\]](#cite_note-UNtwspop-258) In 2008 the leading cause of premature death was [cancer](http://fakehost/wiki/Cancer%20%22Cancer%22), at 29.8%, followed by [ischaemic heart disease](http://fakehost/wiki/Ischaemic_heart_disease%20%22Ischaemic%20heart%20disease%22), 19.7%, and then [cerebrovascular disease](http://fakehost/wiki/Cerebrovascular_disease%20%22Cerebrovascular%20disease%22), 9.2%.[\[252\]](#cite_note-260) As of 2016, total expenditure on [health care](http://fakehost/wiki/Health_care_in_New_Zealand%20%22Health%20care%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (including private sector spending) is 9.2% of GDP.[\[253\]](#cite_note-261)

### Ethnicity and immigration

[![Pedestrians crossing a wide street which is flanked by storefronts](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Queen_Street_Midtown_Auckland.jpg/220px-Queen_Street_Midtown_Auckland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Street_Midtown_Auckland.jpg)

Pedestrians on [Queen Street](http://fakehost/wiki/Queen_Street,_Auckland%20%22Queen%20Street,%20Auckland%22) in Auckland, an ethnically diverse city

In the 2013 census, 74.0% of New Zealand residents identified ethnically as European, and 14.9% as Māori. Other major ethnic groups include [Asian](http://fakehost/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders%20%22Asian%20New%20Zealanders%22) (11.8%) and Pacific peoples (7.4%), two-thirds of whom live in the [Auckland Region](http://fakehost/wiki/Auckland_Region%20%22Auckland%20Region%22).[\[255\]](#cite_note-QuickStats_ethnicity-263)
[\[n 3\]](#cite_note-ethnicity-6) The population has become more diverse in recent decades: in 1961, the census reported that the population of New Zealand was 92% European and 7% Māori, with Asian and Pacific minorities sharing the remaining 1%.[\[256\]](#cite_note-264)

While the [demonym](http://fakehost/wiki/Demonym%20%22Demonym%22) for a New Zealand citizen is New Zealander, the informal "[Kiwi](http://fakehost/wiki/Kiwi_/(people/) "Kiwi (people)")" is commonly used both internationally[\[257\]](#cite_note-265) and by locals.[\[258\]](#cite_note-266) The Māori loanword [Pākehā](http://fakehost/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81%20%22P%C4%81keh%C4%81%22) has been used to refer to [New Zealanders of European descent](http://fakehost/wiki/European_New_Zealanders%20%22European%20New%20Zealanders%22), although others reject this appellation.[\[259\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBain200631-267)
[\[260\]](#cite_note-268) The word Pākehā today is increasingly used to refer to all non-Polynesian New Zealanders.[\[261\]](#cite_note-Pakeha-269)

The Māori were the first people to reach New Zealand, followed by the early [European settlers](http://fakehost/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81_settlers%20%22P%C4%81keh%C4%81%20settlers%22). Following colonisation, immigrants were predominantly from Britain, Ireland and Australia because of restrictive policies similar to the [White Australia policy](http://fakehost/wiki/White_Australia_policy%20%22White%20Australia%20policy%22).[\[262\]](#cite_note-270) There was also significant Dutch, [Dalmatian](http://fakehost/wiki/Dalmatia%20%22Dalmatia%22),[\[263\]](#cite_note-271) German, and Italian immigration, together with indirect European immigration through Australia, North America, South America and South Africa.[\[264\]](#cite_note-272)
[\[265\]](#cite_note-Immigration_history-273) Net migration increased after the Second World War; in the 1970s and 1980s policies were relaxed and immigration from Asia was promoted.[\[265\]](#cite_note-Immigration_history-273)
[\[266\]](#cite_note-274) In 2009–10, an annual target of 45,000–50,000 permanent residence approvals was set by the New Zealand Immigration Service—more than one new migrant for every 100 New Zealand residents.[\[267\]](#cite_note-Recent_immigration-275) Just over 25% of New Zealand's population was born overseas, with the majority (52%) living in the Auckland Region. The United Kingdom remains the largest source of New Zealand's overseas population, with a quarter of all overseas-born New Zealanders born there; other major sources of New Zealand's overseas-born population are China, India, Australia, South Africa, Fiji and Samoa.[\[268\]](#cite_note-276) The number of fee-paying [international students](http://fakehost/wiki/International_student%20%22International%20student%22) increased sharply in the late 1990s, with more than 20,000 studying in public [tertiary institutions](http://fakehost/wiki/Tertiary_education%20%22Tertiary%20education%22) in 2002.[\[269\]](#cite_note-277)

### Language

[![Map of New Zealand showing the percentage of people in each census area unit who speak Māori. Areas of the North Island exhibit the highest Māori proficiency.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/TeReoMaori2013.png/220px-TeReoMaori2013.png)](/wiki/File:TeReoMaori2013.png)

Speakers of Māori according to the 2013 census[\[270\]](#cite_note-278)

  Less than 5%

  More than 5%

  More than 10%

  More than 20%

  More than 30%

  More than 40%

  More than 50%

English is the predominant language in New Zealand, spoken by 96.1% of the population.[\[271\]](#cite_note-SpokenLanguage-279) [New Zealand English](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_English%20%22New%20Zealand%20English%22) is similar to [Australian English](http://fakehost/wiki/Australian_English%20%22Australian%20English%22) and many speakers from the Northern Hemisphere are unable to tell the [accents](http://fakehost/wiki/Accent_/(sociolinguistics/) "Accent (sociolinguistics)") apart.[\[272\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayMaclaganGordon200814-280) The most prominent differences between the New Zealand English dialect and other English dialects are the shifts in the short front vowels: the short-"i" sound (as in "kit") has centralised towards the [schwa](http://fakehost/wiki/Schwa%20%22Schwa%22) sound (the "a" in "comma" and "about"); the short-"e" sound (as in "dress") has moved towards the short-"i" sound; and the short-"a" sound (as in "trap") has moved to the short-"e" sound.[\[273\]](#cite_note-281)

After the Second World War, Māori were discouraged from speaking their own language (_[te reo Māori](http://fakehost/wiki/Te_reo_M%C4%81ori%20%22Te%20reo%20M%C4%81ori%22)_) in schools and workplaces and it existed as a community language only in a few remote areas.[\[274\]](#cite_note-Maori_language-282) It has recently undergone a process of revitalisation,[\[275\]](#cite_note-283) being declared one of New Zealand's official languages in 1987,[\[276\]](#cite_note-284) and is spoken by 3.7% of the population.[\[271\]](#cite_note-SpokenLanguage-279)
[\[n 9\]](#cite_note-286) There are now Māori language immersion schools and two television channels that broadcast predominantly in Māori.[\[278\]](#cite_note-287) [Many places](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_dual_place_names_in_New_Zealand%20%22List%20of%20dual%20place%20names%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) have both their Māori and English names officially recognised.[\[279\]](#cite_note-288)

As recorded in the 2013 census,[\[271\]](#cite_note-SpokenLanguage-279) [Samoan](http://fakehost/wiki/Samoan_language%20%22Samoan%20language%22) is the most widely spoken non-official language (2.2%),[\[n 10\]](#cite_note-289) followed by [Hindi](http://fakehost/wiki/Hindi%20%22Hindi%22) (1.7%), "Northern Chinese" (including [Mandarin](http://fakehost/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese%20%22Mandarin%20Chinese%22), 1.3%) and French (1.2%). 20,235 people (0.5%) reported the ability to use [New Zealand Sign Language](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language%20%22New%20Zealand%20Sign%20Language%22). It was declared one of New Zealand's official languages in 2006.[\[280\]](#cite_note-290)

### Religion

[![Simple white building with two red domed towers](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Ratana_Church_Raetihi.jpg/170px-Ratana_Church_Raetihi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ratana_Church_Raetihi.jpg)

A [Rātana](http://fakehost/wiki/R%C4%81tana%20%22R%C4%81tana%22) church on a hill near [Raetihi](http://fakehost/wiki/Raetihi%20%22Raetihi%22). The two-tower construction is characteristic of Rātana buildings.

[Christianity](http://fakehost/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand%20%22Christianity%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) is the predominant religion in New Zealand, although its society is among the most [secular](http://fakehost/wiki/Secular%20%22Secular%22) in the world.[\[281\]](#cite_note-291)
[\[282\]](#cite_note-292) In the 2018 census, 51.4% of the population identified with one or more religions, including 38.6% identifying as Christians. Another 48.6% indicated that they had no religion.[\[n 11\]](#cite_note-293) The main Christian denominations are, by number of adherents, [Roman Catholicism](http://fakehost/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_New_Zealand%20%22Catholic%20Church%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (10.1%), [Anglicanism](http://fakehost/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_Aotearoa,_New_Zealand_and_Polynesia%20%22Anglican%20Church%20in%20Aotearoa,%20New%20Zealand%20and%20Polynesia%22) (6.8%), [Presbyterianism](http://fakehost/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Aotearoa_New_Zealand%20%22Presbyterian%20Church%20of%20Aotearoa%20New%20Zealand%22) (5.5%) and "Christian not further defined" (i.e. people identifying as Christian but not stating the denomination, 6.6%). The Māori-based [Ringatū](http://fakehost/wiki/Ringat%C5%AB%20%22Ringat%C5%AB%22) and [Rātana](http://fakehost/wiki/R%C4%81tana%20%22R%C4%81tana%22) religions (1.3%) are also Christian in origin.[\[284\]](#cite_note-2013statstables-295)
[\[285\]](#cite_note-296) Immigration and demographic change in recent decades has contributed to the growth of minority religions,[\[286\]](#cite_note-297) such as [Hinduism](http://fakehost/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand%20%22Hinduism%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (2.6%), [Buddhism](http://fakehost/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand%20%22Buddhism%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (1.1%), [Islam](http://fakehost/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand%20%22Islam%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (1.3%) and [Sikhism](http://fakehost/wiki/Sikhism_in_New_Zealand%20%22Sikhism%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) (0.5%).[\[284\]](#cite_note-2013statstables-295) The Auckland Region exhibited the greatest religious diversity.[\[284\]](#cite_note-2013statstables-295)

### Education

Primary and secondary schooling is compulsory for children aged 6 to 16, with the majority attending from the age of 5.[\[287\]](#cite_note-Education_Stats-298) There are 13 school years and attending [state (public) schools](http://fakehost/wiki/State_school%20%22State%20school%22) is free to New Zealand citizens and permanent residents from a person's 5th birthday to the end of the calendar year following their 19th birthday.[\[288\]](#cite_note-299) New Zealand has an adult literacy rate of 99%,[\[143\]](#cite_note-CIA-150) and over half of the population aged 15 to 29 hold a tertiary qualification.[\[287\]](#cite_note-Education_Stats-298) There are five types of government-owned tertiary institutions: universities, colleges of education, [polytechnics](http://fakehost/wiki/Institute_of_technology#New_Zealand%20%22Institute%20of%20technology%22), specialist colleges, and [wānanga](http://fakehost/wiki/W%C4%81nanga%20%22W%C4%81nanga%22),[\[289\]](#cite_note-EducationAct1989-300) in addition to private training establishments.[\[290\]](#cite_note-NZQA-301) In the adult population 14.2% have a [bachelor's degree](http://fakehost/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree%20%22Bachelor's%20degree%22) or higher, 30.4% have some form of secondary qualification as their highest qualification and 22.4% have no formal qualification.[\[291\]](#cite_note-TertiaryEdStats-302) The OECD's [Programme for International Student Assessment](http://fakehost/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment%20%22Programme%20for%20International%20Student%20Assessment%22) ranks New Zealand's education system as the seventh best in the world, with students performing exceptionally well in reading, mathematics and science.[\[292\]](#cite_note-303)

Culture
-------

[![Tall wooden carving showing Kupe above two tentacled sea creatures](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/KupeWheke.jpg/150px-KupeWheke.jpg)](/wiki/File:KupeWheke.jpg "Use the scrollbar to see the full image.")

Late 20th-century house-post depicting the navigator [Kupe](http://fakehost/wiki/Kupe%20%22Kupe%22) fighting two sea creatures

Early Māori adapted the tropically based east [Polynesian culture](http://fakehost/wiki/Polynesian_culture%20%22Polynesian%20culture%22) in line with the challenges associated with a larger and more diverse environment, eventually developing their own distinctive culture. Social organisation was largely communal with families (_[whānau](http://fakehost/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau%20%22Wh%C4%81nau%22)_), subtribes (_hapū_) and tribes (_iwi_) ruled by a chief (_[rangatira](http://fakehost/wiki/Rangatira%20%22Rangatira%22)_), whose position was subject to the community's approval.[\[293\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007398-304) The British and Irish immigrants brought aspects of their own culture to New Zealand and also influenced Māori culture,[\[294\]](#cite_note-305)
[\[295\]](#cite_note-306) particularly with the introduction of Christianity.[\[296\]](#cite_note-307) However, Māori still regard their allegiance to tribal groups as a vital part of [their identity](http://fakehost/wiki/Cultural_identity%20%22Cultural%20identity%22), and Māori kinship roles resemble [those of other Polynesian peoples](http://fakehost/wiki/Hawaiian_kinship%20%22Hawaiian%20kinship%22).[\[297\]](#cite_note-308) More recently [American](http://fakehost/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_States%20%22Culture%20of%20the%20United%20States%22), [Australian](http://fakehost/wiki/Culture_of_Australia%20%22Culture%20of%20Australia%22), [Asian](http://fakehost/wiki/Culture_of_Asia%20%22Culture%20of%20Asia%22) and other [European cultures](http://fakehost/wiki/Culture_of_Europe%20%22Culture%20of%20Europe%22) have exerted influence on New Zealand. Non-Māori Polynesian cultures are also apparent, with [Pasifika](http://fakehost/wiki/Pasifika_Festival%20%22Pasifika%20Festival%22), the world's largest Polynesian festival, now an annual event in Auckland.[\[298\]](#cite_note-309)

The largely rural life in early New Zealand led to the image of New Zealanders being rugged, industrious problem solvers.[\[299\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007400-310) Modesty was expected and enforced through the "[tall poppy syndrome](http://fakehost/wiki/Tall_poppy_syndrome%20%22Tall%20poppy%20syndrome%22)", where high achievers received harsh criticism.[\[300\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007399-311) At the time New Zealand was not known as an intellectual country.[\[301\]](#cite_note-312) From the early 20th century until the late 1960s, Māori culture was suppressed by the attempted [assimilation](http://fakehost/wiki/Forced_assimilation%20%22Forced%20assimilation%22) of Māori into British New Zealanders.[\[274\]](#cite_note-Maori_language-282) In the 1960s, as tertiary education became more available and [cities expanded](http://fakehost/wiki/Urbanization%20%22Urbanization%22)
[\[302\]](#cite_note-313) urban culture began to dominate.[\[303\]](#cite_note-314) However, rural imagery and themes are common in New Zealand's art, literature and media.[\[304\]](#cite_note-315)

[New Zealand's national symbols](http://fakehost/wiki/National_symbols_of_New_Zealand%20%22National%20symbols%20of%20New%20Zealand%22) are influenced by natural, historical, and Māori sources. The [silver fern](http://fakehost/wiki/Silver_fern%20%22Silver%20fern%22) is an emblem appearing on army insignia and sporting team uniforms.[\[305\]](#cite_note-Wilson2016-316) Certain items of popular culture thought to be unique to New Zealand are called "[Kiwiana](http://fakehost/wiki/Kiwiana%20%22Kiwiana%22)".[\[305\]](#cite_note-Wilson2016-316)

### Art

As part of the resurgence of Māori culture, the traditional crafts of carving and weaving are now more widely practised and Māori artists are increasing in number and influence.[\[306\]](#cite_note-Creative-317) Most Māori carvings feature human figures, generally with three fingers and either a natural-looking, detailed head or a grotesque head.[\[307\]](#cite_note-318) Surface patterns consisting of spirals, ridges, notches and fish scales decorate most carvings.[\[308\]](#cite_note-319) The pre-eminent Māori architecture consisted of carved meeting houses (_[wharenui](http://fakehost/wiki/Wharenui%20%22Wharenui%22)_) decorated with symbolic carvings and illustrations. These buildings were originally designed to be constantly rebuilt, changing and adapting to different whims or needs.[\[309\]](#cite_note-320)

Māori decorated the white wood of buildings, canoes and cenotaphs using red (a mixture of red [ochre](http://fakehost/wiki/Ochre%20%22Ochre%22) and shark fat) and black (made from soot) paint and painted pictures of birds, reptiles and other designs on cave walls.[\[310\]](#cite_note-321) Māori tattoos (_[moko](http://fakehost/wiki/T%C4%81_moko%20%22T%C4%81%20moko%22)_) consisting of coloured soot mixed with gum were cut into the flesh with a bone chisel.[\[311\]](#cite_note-322) Since European arrival paintings and photographs have been dominated by landscapes, originally not as works of art but as factual portrayals of New Zealand.[\[312\]](#cite_note-beginnings-323) Portraits of Māori were also common, with early painters often portraying them as "[noble savages](http://fakehost/wiki/Noble_savage%20%22Noble%20savage%22)", exotic beauties or friendly natives.[\[312\]](#cite_note-beginnings-323) The country's isolation delayed the influence of European artistic trends allowing local artists to develop their own distinctive style of [regionalism](http://fakehost/wiki/Regionalism_/(art/) "Regionalism (art)").[\[313\]](#cite_note-324) During the 1960s and 1970s many artists combined traditional Māori and Western techniques, creating unique art forms.[\[314\]](#cite_note-325) New Zealand art and craft has gradually achieved an international audience, with exhibitions in the [Venice Biennale](http://fakehost/wiki/Venice_Biennale%20%22Venice%20Biennale%22) in 2001 and the "Paradise Now" exhibition in New York in 2004.[\[306\]](#cite_note-Creative-317)
[\[315\]](#cite_note-326)

[![Refer to caption](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Hinepare.jpg/170px-Hinepare.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hinepare.jpg)

Māori cloaks are made of fine flax fibre and patterned with black, red and white triangles, diamonds and other geometric shapes.[\[316\]](#cite_note-327) [Greenstone](http://fakehost/wiki/Pounamu%20%22Pounamu%22) was fashioned into earrings and necklaces, with the most well-known design being the [hei-tiki](http://fakehost/wiki/Hei-tiki%20%22Hei-tiki%22), a distorted human figure sitting cross-legged with its head tilted to the side.[\[317\]](#cite_note-328) Europeans brought English fashion etiquette to New Zealand, and until the 1950s most people dressed up for social occasions.[\[318\]](#cite_note-329) Standards have since relaxed and New Zealand fashion has received a reputation for being casual, practical and lacklustre.[\[319\]](#cite_note-330)
[\[320\]](#cite_note-The_Economist_print_edition-331) However, the local fashion industry has grown significantly since 2000, doubling exports and increasing from a handful to about 50 established labels, with some labels gaining international recognition.[\[320\]](#cite_note-The_Economist_print_edition-331)

### Literature

Māori quickly adopted writing as a means of sharing ideas, and many of their oral stories and poems were converted to the written form.[\[321\]](#cite_note-332) Most early English literature was obtained from Britain and it was not until the 1950s when local publishing outlets increased that New Zealand literature started to become widely known.[\[322\]](#cite_note-333) Although still largely influenced by global trends ([modernism](http://fakehost/wiki/Literary_modernism%20%22Literary%20modernism%22)) and events (the Great Depression), writers in the 1930s began to develop stories increasingly focused on their experiences in New Zealand. During this period literature changed from a [journalistic](http://fakehost/wiki/Journalism%20%22Journalism%22) activity to a more academic pursuit.[\[323\]](#cite_note-334) Participation in the world wars gave some New Zealand writers a new perspective on New Zealand culture and with the post-war expansion of universities local literature flourished.[\[324\]](#cite_note-335) Dunedin is a UNESCO [City of Literature](http://fakehost/wiki/City_of_Literature%20%22City%20of%20Literature%22).[\[325\]](#cite_note-336)

### Media and entertainment

New Zealand music has been influenced by [blues](http://fakehost/wiki/Blues%20%22Blues%22), [jazz](http://fakehost/wiki/Jazz%20%22Jazz%22), [country](http://fakehost/wiki/Country_music%20%22Country%20music%22), [rock and roll](http://fakehost/wiki/Rock_and_roll%20%22Rock%20and%20roll%22) and [hip hop](http://fakehost/wiki/Hip_hop_music%20%22Hip%20hop%20music%22), with many of these genres given a unique New Zealand interpretation.[\[326\]](#cite_note-music-337) Māori developed traditional chants and songs from their ancient Southeast Asian origins, and after centuries of isolation created a unique "monotonous" and "[doleful](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/doleful "wikt:doleful")" sound.[\[327\]](#cite_note-338) Flutes and trumpets were used as musical instruments[\[328\]](#cite_note-339) or as signalling devices during war or special occasions.[\[329\]](#cite_note-340) Early settlers brought over their ethnic music, with [brass bands](http://fakehost/wiki/Brass_band%20%22Brass%20band%22) and [choral music](http://fakehost/wiki/Choir%20%22Choir%22) being popular, and musicians began touring New Zealand in the 1860s.[\[330\]](#cite_note-341)
[\[331\]](#cite_note-342) [Pipe bands](http://fakehost/wiki/Pipe_band%20%22Pipe%20band%22) became widespread during the early 20th century.[\[332\]](#cite_note-343) The New Zealand recording industry began to develop from 1940 onwards and many New Zealand musicians have obtained success in Britain and the United States.[\[326\]](#cite_note-music-337) Some artists release Māori language songs and the Māori tradition-based art of _[kapa haka](http://fakehost/wiki/Kapa_haka%20%22Kapa%20haka%22)_ (song and dance) has made a resurgence.[\[333\]](#cite_note-344) The [New Zealand Music Awards](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Music_Awards%20%22New%20Zealand%20Music%20Awards%22) are held annually by [Recorded Music NZ](http://fakehost/wiki/Recorded_Music_NZ%20%22Recorded%20Music%20NZ%22); the awards were first held in 1965 by [Reckitt & Colman](http://fakehost/wiki/Reckitt_%26_Colman%20%22Reckitt%20&%20Colman%22) as the [Loxene Golden Disc](http://fakehost/wiki/Loxene_Golden_Disc%20%22Loxene%20Golden%20Disc%22) awards.[\[334\]](#cite_note-345) Recorded Music NZ also publishes the country's [official weekly record charts](http://fakehost/wiki/Official_New_Zealand_Music_Chart%20%22Official%20New%20Zealand%20Music%20Chart%22).[\[335\]](#cite_note-346)

[![Hills with inset, round doors. Reflected in water.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Hobbit_holes_reflected_in_water.jpg/220px-Hobbit_holes_reflected_in_water.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hobbit_holes_reflected_in_water.jpg)

Public [radio](http://fakehost/wiki/Radio_in_New_Zealand%20%22Radio%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) was introduced in New Zealand in 1922.[\[337\]](#cite_note-348) A state-owned [television service](http://fakehost/wiki/Television_in_New_Zealand%20%22Television%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) began in 1960.[\[338\]](#cite_note-349) Deregulation in the 1980s saw a sudden increase in the numbers of radio and television stations.[\[339\]](#cite_note-NZ_TV-350) New Zealand television primarily broadcasts American and British programming, along with a large number of Australian and local shows.[\[340\]](#cite_note-351) The number of [New Zealand films](http://fakehost/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_films%20%22List%20of%20New%20Zealand%20films%22) significantly increased during the 1970s. In 1978 the [New Zealand Film Commission](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_Film_Commission%20%22New%20Zealand%20Film%20Commission%22) started assisting local film-makers and many films attained a world audience, some receiving international acknowledgement.[\[339\]](#cite_note-NZ_TV-350) The highest-grossing New Zealand films are _[Hunt for the Wilderpeople](http://fakehost/wiki/Hunt_for_the_Wilderpeople%20%22Hunt%20for%20the%20Wilderpeople%22)_, _[Boy](http://fakehost/wiki/Boy_/(2010_film/) "Boy (2010 film)")_, _[The World's Fastest Indian](http://fakehost/wiki/The_World%27s_Fastest_Indian%20%22The%20World's%20Fastest%20Indian%22)_, _[Once Were Warriors](http://fakehost/wiki/Once_Were_Warriors_/(film/) "Once Were Warriors (film)")_ and _[Whale Rider](http://fakehost/wiki/Whale_Rider%20%22Whale%20Rider%22)_.[\[341\]](#cite_note-352) The country's diverse scenery and compact size, plus government incentives,[\[342\]](#cite_note-353) have encouraged some [producers](http://fakehost/wiki/Film_producer%20%22Film%20producer%22) to shoot big-budget productions in New Zealand, including _[Avatar](http://fakehost/wiki/Avatar_/(2009_film/) "Avatar (2009 film)")_, _[The Lord of the Rings](http://fakehost/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_/(film_series/) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)")_, _[The Hobbit](http://fakehost/wiki/The_Hobbit_/(film_series/) "The Hobbit (film series)")_, _[The Chronicles of Narnia](http://fakehost/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia_/(film_series/) "The Chronicles of Narnia (film series)")_, _[King Kong](http://fakehost/wiki/King_Kong_/(2005_film/) "King Kong (2005 film)")_ and _[The Last Samurai](http://fakehost/wiki/The_Last_Samurai%20%22The%20Last%20Samurai%22)_.[\[343\]](#cite_note-354) The New Zealand media industry is dominated by a small number of companies, most of which are foreign-owned, although the [state retains ownership](http://fakehost/wiki/Crown_entity%20%22Crown%20entity%22) of some television and radio stations.[\[344\]](#cite_note-355) Since 1994, [Freedom House](http://fakehost/wiki/Freedom_House%20%22Freedom%20House%22) has consistently ranked New Zealand's press freedom in the top twenty, with the 19th freest media in 2015.[\[345\]](#cite_note-356)

### Sports

[![Rugby team wearing all black, facing the camera, knees bent, and facing toward a team wearing white](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Haka_2006.jpg/220px-Haka_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haka_2006.jpg)

Most of the major sporting codes played in New Zealand have British origins.[\[346\]](#cite_note-357) [Rugby union](http://fakehost/wiki/Rugby_union%20%22Rugby%20union%22) is considered the [national sport](http://fakehost/wiki/National_sport%20%22National%20sport%22)
[\[347\]](#cite_note-358) and attracts the most spectators.[\[348\]](#cite_note-Organised_Sport-359) [Golf](http://fakehost/wiki/Golf%20%22Golf%22), [netball](http://fakehost/wiki/Netball%20%22Netball%22), [tennis](http://fakehost/wiki/Tennis%20%22Tennis%22) and [cricket](http://fakehost/wiki/Cricket%20%22Cricket%22) have the highest rates of adult participation, while netball, rugby union and [football (soccer)](http://fakehost/wiki/Association_football%20%22Association%20football%22) are particularly popular among young people.[\[348\]](#cite_note-Organised_Sport-359)
[\[349\]](#cite_note-nzsssc-360) Around 54% of New Zealand adolescents participate in sports for their school.[\[349\]](#cite_note-nzsssc-360) Victorious rugby tours to Australia and the United Kingdom in the [late 1880s](http://fakehost/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_New_Zealand_Native_football_team%20%221888%E2%80%9389%20New%20Zealand%20Native%20football%20team%22) and the [early 1900s](http://fakehost/wiki/The_Original_All_Blacks%20%22The%20Original%20All%20Blacks%22) played an early role in instilling a national identity.[\[350\]](#cite_note-361) [Horseracing](http://fakehost/wiki/Horseracing_in_New_Zealand%20%22Horseracing%20in%20New%20Zealand%22) was also a popular [spectator sport](http://fakehost/wiki/Spectator_sport%20%22Spectator%20sport%22) and became part of the "Rugby, Racing and Beer" culture during the 1960s.[\[351\]](#cite_note-362) Māori participation in European sports was particularly evident in rugby and the country's team performs a [haka](http://fakehost/wiki/Haka_/(sports/) "Haka (sports)"), a traditional Māori challenge, before international matches.[\[352\]](#cite_note-363) New Zealand is known for its [extreme sports](http://fakehost/wiki/Extreme_sport%20%22Extreme%20sport%22), [adventure tourism](http://fakehost/wiki/Adventure_travel%20%22Adventure%20travel%22)
[\[353\]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBain200669-364) and strong [mountaineering](http://fakehost/wiki/Mountaineering%20%22Mountaineering%22) tradition, as seen in the success of notable New Zealander [Sir Edmund Hillary](http://fakehost/wiki/Edmund_Hillary%20%22Edmund%20Hillary%22).[\[354\]](#cite_note-365)
[\[355\]](#cite_note-366) Other outdoor pursuits such as [cycling](http://fakehost/wiki/Cycling_in_New_Zealand%20%22Cycling%20in%20New%20Zealand%22), fishing, swimming, running, [tramping](http://fakehost/wiki/Tramping_in_New_Zealand%20%22Tramping%20in%20New%20Zealand%22), canoeing, hunting, snowsports, surfing and sailing are also popular.[\[356\]](#cite_note-SportsParticipation-367) The Polynesian sport of [waka ama](http://fakehost/wiki/Waka_ama%20%22Waka%20ama%22) racing has experienced a resurgence of interest in New Zealand since the 1980s.[\[357\]](#cite_note-368)

New Zealand has competitive international teams in [rugby union](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team%20%22New%20Zealand%20national%20rugby%20union%20team%22), [rugby league](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team%20%22New%20Zealand%20national%20rugby%20league%20team%22), [netball](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_national_netball_team%20%22New%20Zealand%20national%20netball%20team%22), [cricket](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_national_cricket_team%20%22New%20Zealand%20national%20cricket%20team%22), [softball](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_softball_team%20%22New%20Zealand%20men's%20national%20softball%20team%22), and [sailing](http://fakehost/wiki/Team_New_Zealand%20%22Team%20New%20Zealand%22). New Zealand participated at the [Summer Olympics](http://fakehost/wiki/Summer_Olympics%20%22Summer%20Olympics%22) in 1908 and 1912 as [a joint team with Australia](http://fakehost/wiki/Australasia_at_the_Olympics%20%22Australasia%20at%20the%20Olympics%22), before first participating [on its own](http://fakehost/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_Olympics%20%22New%20Zealand%20at%20the%20Olympics%22) in 1920.[\[358\]](#cite_note-369) The country has ranked highly on a medals-to-population ratio at recent Games.[\[359\]](#cite_note-370)
[\[360\]](#cite_note-371) The "All Blacks", the national rugby union team, are the most successful in the history of international rugby[\[361\]](#cite_note-372) and the reigning [World Cup](http://fakehost/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup%20%22Rugby%20World%20Cup%22) champions.[\[362\]](#cite_note-373)

### Cuisine

[![Raw meat and vegetables](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Hangi_ingredients.jpg/220px-Hangi_ingredients.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hangi_ingredients.jpg)

Ingredients to be prepared for a [hāngi](http://fakehost/wiki/H%C4%81ngi%20%22H%C4%81ngi%22)

The national cuisine has been described as [Pacific Rim](http://fakehost/wiki/Pacific_Rim%20%22Pacific%20Rim%22), incorporating the native [Māori cuisine](http://fakehost/wiki/M%C4%81ori_cuisine%20%22M%C4%81ori%20cuisine%22) and diverse culinary traditions introduced by settlers and immigrants from Europe, Polynesia and Asia.[\[363\]](#cite_note-cuisine-374) New Zealand yields produce from land and sea—most crops and livestock, such as maize, potatoes and pigs, were gradually introduced by the early European settlers.[\[364\]](#cite_note-375) Distinctive ingredients or dishes include [lamb](http://fakehost/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton%20%22Lamb%20and%20mutton%22), salmon, [kōura](http://fakehost/wiki/K%C5%8Dura%20%22K%C5%8Dura%22) (crayfish),[\[365\]](#cite_note-376) [dredge oysters](http://fakehost/wiki/Dredge_oyster%20%22Dredge%20oyster%22), [whitebait](http://fakehost/wiki/Whitebait%20%22Whitebait%22), [pāua](http://fakehost/wiki/P%C4%81ua%20%22P%C4%81ua%22) (abalone), mussels, scallops, [pipis](http://fakehost/wiki/Paphies_australis%20%22Paphies%20australis%22) and [tuatua](http://fakehost/wiki/Tuatua%20%22Tuatua%22) (both are types of New Zealand shellfish),[\[366\]](#cite_note-377) [kūmara](http://fakehost/wiki/K%C5%ABmara%20%22K%C5%ABmara%22) (sweet potato), [kiwifruit](http://fakehost/wiki/Kiwifruit%20%22Kiwifruit%22), [tamarillo](http://fakehost/wiki/Tamarillo%20%22Tamarillo%22) and [pavlova](http://fakehost/wiki/Pavlova_/(food/) "Pavlova (food)") (considered a national dish).[\[367\]](#cite_note-378)
[\[363\]](#cite_note-cuisine-374) A [hāngi](http://fakehost/wiki/H%C4%81ngi%20%22H%C4%81ngi%22) is a traditional Māori method of cooking food using heated rocks buried in a pit oven. After European colonisation, Māori began cooking with pots and ovens and the hāngi was used less frequently, although it is still used for formal occasions such as [tangihanga](http://fakehost/wiki/Tangihanga%20%22Tangihanga%22).[\[368\]](#cite_note-379)
